standards.standard_description: Ask and answer questions about key details in a text
standards.parent_tip: After reading a story together, ask your child who, what, where, when, and why questions to practice finding key details.

questions.prompt: After Bella picked the apples, what did she do next?
choices: She put them in her basket.
She ate them for a snack.
She left them on the tree.
She gave them to her mom.
questions.correct: She put them in her basket.
questions.hint: Look at the second sentence of the story to find out what Bella did with the apples.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! After Bella picked the apples, she put them in her basket. Nice work!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Hmm, think about what Bella did right after she picked the apples. Where would she put them? Try again!
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! After she picked them, Bella put the apples in her basket. Now you know!

questions.prompt:
The frog sat on a lily pad in the pond. He was waiting for a fly.

Where was the frog sitting?
questions.hint: Look at the first sentence — where did the frog sit?
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! The frog sat on a lily pad in the pond. Nice work!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Good try! Read the story again. It tells you just where the frog sat. Look for that spot!
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! The frog was sitting on a lily pad. Now you know!

questions.prompt:
Emma walked to school with her friend Jake. They talked about their favorite animals.

Who did Emma walk with?
questions.hint: Read the first sentence — who walked with Emma?
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! Emma walked to school with her friend Jake. Nice work!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Almost! Look back at the story. It says who walked with Emma. Read it one more time!
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! Emma walked with her friend Jake. Now you know!

questions.prompt:
Dad made pancakes for breakfast. He put blueberries on top.

What did Dad put on the pancakes?
questions.hint: Read the second sentence — what went on top?
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! Dad put blueberries on top of the pancakes. Nice work!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Close one! Read the story again. It tells you just what Dad put on top. Look for it!
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! Dad put blueberries on the pancakes. Now you know!

questions.prompt: Listen to the story: A kitten climbed up a tall tree. It went very high and got stuck. It could not get back down, so it cried and cried. Why was the kitten scared?
choices: It saw a big, scary dog.
It was sleepy and wanted to nap.
It could not get down from the tall tree.
It wanted to climb even higher.
questions.correct: It could not get down from the tall tree.
questions.hint: Think about where the kitten was and what it was trying to do.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! The kitten was scared because it could not get down from the tall tree. Nice work!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Take another look! The kitten cried and cried. Think about what was wrong up in that tree.
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! The kitten was scared because it could not get down from the tall tree. Now you know!

questions.prompt:
Sam ran to the window and put on his boots. He grabbed his umbrella and smiled. Then he ran outside to jump.

What was the weather like when Sam went outside?
questions.hint: The story never says the weather. Look at the things Sam grabbed and ask what kind of day you need those for.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! Sam grabbed his umbrella and boots, so it was raining. Nice work!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Hmm, think about it. Why would Sam need boots and an umbrella? What kind of weather is that?
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! It was raining. His umbrella and boots were the clues. Now you know!

questions.prompt:
Mia had two cookies on her plate. She gave one to her little brother. When she looked back, her plate was empty and the dog was licking his lips.

Who ate Mia's last cookie?
questions.hint: Count what happened to each cookie. One cookie is explained. Then find the clue about the one that is missing.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! The dog was licking his lips, so the dog ate the cookie. Nice work!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Good try! Look for the clue. Who was licking their lips by the empty plate? Read again!
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! The dog ate the last cookie. The licking lips gave it away. Now you know!

questions.prompt:
At the park, Ben's balloon was not in his hand anymore. He looked up at the sky. His face was sad and he began to cry.

Why is Ben crying?
choices: He is tired and wants a nap.
He does not like the park.
He fell down and got hurt.
questions.hint: Put the clues together: where his balloon is now, and where he is looking. What happened to it?
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! Ben's balloon was not in his hand and he looked at the sky. It floated away! Nice work!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Almost! Why was Ben looking up at the sky? Think about what left his hand.
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! Ben is crying because his balloon floated away. Now you know!

questions.prompt:
Lily planted a seed on Monday and gave it water. Every morning she checked the pot. On Monday there was dirt. On Friday there was a tiny green leaf.

What happened to the seed during the week?
questions.hint: Compare what the pot looked like on Monday to what it looked like on Friday. What changed?
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! First there was dirt, then a tiny green leaf. The seed started to grow! Nice work!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Close one! On Monday there was dirt, and on Friday a green leaf. What happened to the seed?
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! The seed started to grow. The tiny leaf was the clue. Now you know!

questions.prompt:
Max wanted to reach the cookie jar on the tall shelf. First he stood on his toes, but he was too short. Then he pushed a chair to the shelf and climbed up. He smiled with a cookie in his hand.

How did Max finally get the cookie?
choices: He climbed up on a chair.
He asked his mom for it.
questions.correct: He climbed up on a chair.
questions.hint: He tried two things. One did not work. Read carefully to find which one worked at the end.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! Max pushed a chair over and climbed up to reach the cookie. Nice work!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Take another look! Standing on his toes did not work. What did Max do next to reach the shelf?
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! Max got the cookie by climbing up on a chair. Now you know!

standards.standard_description: Retell stories, including key details, and demonstrate understanding of their central message or lesson
standards.parent_tip: After reading, ask your child: What happened first? Then what? What did the character learn?

questions.prompt:
Tom wanted to win the race. He practiced running every day. On race day, he came in first!

What is the lesson of this story?
questions.hint: What did Tom do before the race that helped him win?
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! Tom practiced every day and won the race. Practice helps you get better! Nice work!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Hmm, think about what Tom did every day before the race. Why did he win? Try again!
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! The lesson is that practice helps you get better. Now you know!

questions.prompt:
Mia did not share her crayons. Her friends stopped playing with her. Mia felt sad and lonely.

What is the lesson of this story?
questions.hint: What happened when Mia didn't share?
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! Mia did not share, and her friends felt left out. Sharing is important! Nice work!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Good try! Think about what Mia would not do with her crayons. Why did her friends leave?
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! The lesson is that sharing is important. Now you know!

questions.prompt:
The ant worked hard all summer saving food. The grasshopper played all day. When winter came, the ant had food but the grasshopper did not.

What is the lesson?
choices: It is good to plan ahead
questions.correct: It is good to plan ahead
questions.hint: Who was ready for winter and why?
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! The ant saved food for winter and was ready. It is good to plan ahead! Nice work!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Almost! The ant got ready before winter, but the grasshopper played. What is the big lesson?
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! The lesson is that it is good to plan ahead. Now you know!

questions.prompt: Listen to the story: Leo could not ride his bike. He fell down again and again, but he did not give up. He kept trying, and soon he could ride all by himself. What lesson can we learn from Leo's story?
choices: Bikes are only for big kids.
If you keep trying, you can learn new things.
It is fun to fall down.
You should always listen to your mom.
questions.correct: If you keep trying, you can learn new things.
questions.hint: Think about what Leo's mom told him to do and what happened after he followed her advice.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! Leo kept trying and learned to ride his bike. Nice work!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Close one! Leo fell, but he did not give up. Think about what happened when he kept going.
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! The lesson is if you keep trying, you can learn new things. Now you know!

questions.prompt:
Sara told a lie about breaking the vase. She felt bad all day. She told the truth and felt much better.

What is the lesson?
questions.hint: How did Sara feel after she told the truth vs. the lie?
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! Sara felt bad when she lied and better when she told the truth. Nice work!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Take another look! How did Sara feel all day after she told the lie? Think about that.
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! The lesson is that lying makes you feel bad. Now you know!

questions.prompt:
Ben saw a big dog. He wanted to pet it, but his mom said, "Wait. Ask the owner first." Ben asked the owner. The owner smiled and said yes. The dog licked Ben's hand.

What lesson does this story teach?
choices: Ask before you touch someone's pet
Moms know a lot about dogs
questions.correct: Ask before you touch someone's pet
questions.hint: Think about what Ben's mom told him to do, and why it made things go well.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! Ben asked the owner first before petting the dog. Nice work!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Hmm, think about what Ben's mom told him to do first. What did he do before petting?
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! The lesson is to ask before you touch someone's pet. Now you know!

questions.prompt:
Lily planted a seed. Each day she gave it water. Nothing came up. She wanted to dig it up, but she waited. After many days, a small green leaf pushed out of the dirt.

What is the main lesson of this story?
questions.hint: Notice how Lily almost gave up but kept waiting. What did waiting get her?
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! Lily waited many days, and then the leaf grew. Good things can take time! Nice work!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Good try! Lily wanted to dig it up, but she waited. What did waiting teach her?
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! The lesson is that good things can take time. Now you know!

questions.prompt:
Sam broke his sister's toy. He was scared to tell her. He thought about hiding it. Instead, he said, "I broke it. I'm sorry." His sister was still sad, but she gave him a hug.

What does this story mostly teach?
questions.hint: Sam had two choices. Which one did he pick, even though it was hard?
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! Sam was scared but told the truth anyway. Telling the truth is brave! Nice work!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Almost! Sam was scared to tell, but he did not hide it. What big thing did he do?
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! The lesson is that telling the truth is brave. Now you know!

questions.prompt:
Rosa was the fastest runner. In the race, her friend Kim tripped and fell. Rosa stopped running and helped Kim up. They finished the race last, holding hands. Rosa smiled the whole time.

What is the lesson of this story?
choices: Being a good friend matters more than winning
questions.correct: Being a good friend matters more than winning
questions.hint: Rosa could have won, but she chose something else. Why was she still smiling at the end?
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! Rosa stopped to help Kim and finished last, but smiled. Being a good friend matters most! Nice work!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Close one! Rosa could have won, but she stopped to help her friend. Why did she smile?
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! The lesson is that being a good friend matters more than winning. Now you know!

questions.prompt:
Max wanted the last cookie. So did his little brother. Max saw his brother's sad face. Max broke the cookie in two and gave half away. Both boys ate and laughed together.

What lesson does the story teach?
choices: Max is bigger than his brother
questions.hint: Think about what Max did after he saw his brother's face, and how both boys felt at the end.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! Max shared the cookie, and both boys laughed. Sharing makes everyone happy! Nice work!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Take another look! Max broke the cookie in two. How did both boys feel after that?
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! The lesson is that sharing can make everyone happy. Now you know!

standards.standard_description: Describe characters, settings, and major events in a story, using key details
standards.parent_tip: Ask your child: Who is in the story? Where does it happen? What are the big things that happen?

questions.prompt: Listen to the story: An old woman lived in a small house. Her little house sat right next to the ocean. Every morning she watched the waves and the boats. Which words best describe where the old woman lives?
choices: A big house in a forest.
A big house near a mountain.
A small house near the ocean.
A small house in a big city.
questions.correct: A small house near the ocean.
questions.hint: Look at the first sentence to find out about the old woman's home.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! The woman lived in a little house right by the ocean. Nice work!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Hmm, read again. How big was her house, and what was right next to it? Look for both clues!
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! She lives in a small house near the ocean. Now you know!

questions.prompt:
Ben was always helping others. He carried bags for his neighbor and shared his lunch with friends.

What word best describes Ben?
questions.hint: Think about what Ben does — helping and sharing.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! Ben carried bags and shared his lunch, so he is kind. Nice work!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Good try! Think about the nice things Ben did for others. What word matches a helper like that?
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! Ben is kind. His helping was the clue. Now you know!

questions.prompt:
It was a dark and stormy night. The wind howled outside the old barn.

What is the setting?
questions.hint: The setting is WHERE and WHEN the story happens.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! The story happens by an old barn on a dark, stormy night. Nice work!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Almost! Where and when does the story happen? Look for the words about the night and the barn.
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! The setting is a dark, stormy barn. Now you know!

questions.prompt: Listen to the story: First, Rosa found a lost puppy in the grass. Then she gave it some water. At last, she found the puppy's owner. Which major event began the story about Rosa?
questions.hint: Think about the very first thing that happened in the story that made Rosa do other things.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! The story began when Rosa found a lost puppy in the grass. Nice work!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Close one! The word 'first' is a clue. What happened at the very start of Rosa's story?
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! The story began when Rosa found a lost puppy. Now you know!

questions.prompt:
Grumpy Gus never smiled. He frowned at everyone. One day a little girl gave him a flower and he finally smiled.

How does Gus change in the story?
choices: He goes from grumpy to happy
questions.correct: He goes from grumpy to happy
questions.hint: How is Gus different at the end compared to the beginning?
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! Gus never smiled, but the flower made him smile. He goes from grumpy to happy! Nice work!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Take another look! How was Gus at the start? How was he after the girl gave him a flower?
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! Gus goes from grumpy to happy. Now you know!

questions.prompt:
The wind blew Mia's hat into the pond. Her little brother started to cry. Mia did not try to get her hat back. Instead, she hugged him and made a silly face until he smiled.

What does this MOST show about Mia?
questions.hint: Ask yourself WHY Mia let her hat float away. What did she choose to do instead?
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! Mia let her hat go to hug her brother and cheer him up. She is caring! Nice work!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Hmm, think about what Mia did. She left her hat to help her brother. What does that show?
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! This shows Mia is caring. Now you know!

questions.prompt:
Sam pulled on his boots and his thick mittens. He stepped outside. His breath made little white clouds in the air. Not one leaf was on the trees.

When does this story most likely happen?
questions.hint: Put ALL the clues together: mittens, breath you can see, and bare trees. Which season fits every clue?
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! Sam wore mittens and his breath made white clouds, so it is winter. Nice work!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Good try! Think about the clues. Mittens, cold breath, no leaves. What time of year is that?
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! This story happens in winter. Now you know!

questions.prompt:
Rosa put some seeds in a cup of dirt. She set the cup on the sunny windowsill. Every single day, she gave it a little water. One morning, a tiny green leaf poked out of the dirt.

Why did the leaf most likely grow?
choices: Because the dirt got wet one time
Because Rosa watered it and gave it light
Because Rosa put the seeds in a cup
questions.correct: Because Rosa watered it and gave it light
questions.hint: Look for TWO things Rosa did every day. One is told to you, and one is hidden in where she put the cup.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! Rosa gave the seed water every day and set it in the sun. That helped it grow! Nice work!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Almost! What two things did Rosa do every day? Think about the water and the sunny spot.
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! The leaf grew because Rosa watered it and gave it light. Now you know!

questions.prompt:
Leo could not find his dog, Pip. He looked and looked. His tummy felt tight. Then he heard a bark! Pip ran up wagging his tail, and Leo let out a big, slow breath.

How did Leo feel at the BEGINNING, and then at the END?
questions.hint: The story never says how Leo feels. Use the body clues: a 'tight tummy' at the start and a 'big breath' at the end.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! Leo's tummy felt tight at first, then he smiled when Pip came back. Worried, then happy! Nice work!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Close one! How did Leo feel when Pip was lost? How did he feel when Pip ran back? Think of both!
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! Leo felt worried, then happy. Now you know!

questions.prompt:
Three kids raced to the big oak tree. Nia wore a bright red cap. Tom ran the fastest, but he tripped on a root and fell. The kid in the red cap reached out and touched the tree first.

Who won the race?
questions.hint: First find out WHO wore the red cap. Then read again to see who the red cap belonged to at the finish.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! Nia wore the red cap, and the kid in the red cap touched the tree first. Nia won! Nice work!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Take another look! Who touched the tree first? Now, who was wearing the red cap? Match them up!
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! Nia won the race. She wore the red cap. Now you know!

standards.standard_description: Identify words and phrases in stories or poems that suggest feelings or appeal to the senses
standards.parent_tip: Point out feeling words and sensory words when reading together: 'The author says sparkling — can you picture that?'

questions.prompt: The bright sun warmed her face.
questions.hint: Look for the word that tells you how the sun looked in the sentence.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! 'Bright' is the word that tells us about the sun. Nice work!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Hmm, look for the word that tells us more about the sun. How did the sun look?
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! 'Bright' tells us about the sun. Now you know!

questions.prompt:
The puppy whimpered softly in the rain.

How does the puppy probably feel?
questions.hint: Whimpering is a soft, sad sound — like a small cry. What feeling matches that?
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! The puppy whimpered softly in the rain, so it feels sad or scared. Nice work!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Good try! A puppy whimpering in the rain gives a hint. Does that sound happy or not?
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! The puppy probably feels sad or scared. Now you know!

questions.prompt:
The cookies smelled sweet and warm from the oven.

Which words appeal to your sense of smell?
questions.hint: Which words describe how the cookies smell?
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! 'Sweet and warm' are words your nose can smell. Nice work!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Almost! Your nose smells things. Which words tell how the cookies smelled?
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! 'Sweet and warm' appeal to your sense of smell. Now you know!

questions.prompt:
Jake stomped his feet and crossed his arms.

How does Jake feel?
questions.hint: When people stomp and cross their arms, how do they usually feel?
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! Jake stomped his feet and crossed his arms, so he feels angry. Nice work!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Close one! Think about how someone acts when they stomp and cross their arms. How do they feel?
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! Jake feels angry. His stomping was the clue. Now you know!

questions.prompt:
The soft, fluffy blanket kept her cozy all night.

Which words help you imagine how the blanket feels?
questions.hint: Which words describe what the blanket feels like when you touch it?
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! 'Soft and fluffy' help you feel how the blanket feels. Nice work!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Take another look! Which words tell you how the blanket feels when you touch it?
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! 'Soft and fluffy' show how the blanket feels. Now you know!

questions.prompt:
Read the poem.

The rough bark scratched my hand.
A sweet song filled the air.

Which words help you feel something by TOUCH?
questions.hint: Touch words tell how something feels on your skin. Which words could you feel with your hand?
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! 'Rough' and 'scratched' are words you can feel by touch. Nice work!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Hmm, think about touch. Which words tell how the bark felt on your hand?
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! 'Rough, scratched' help you feel by touch. Now you know!

questions.prompt:
The kite dipped down again.
"Not again," sighed Sam, and let go of the string.

How does Sam most likely feel?
questions.hint: Put the clues together: the word 'sighed,' the words 'Not again,' and letting go. What feeling do they all point to?
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! Sam sighed and let go of the string after the kite kept dipping. He feels frustrated! Nice work!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Good try! Sam said 'Not again' and let go of the string. How would you feel then?
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! Sam most likely feels frustrated. Now you know!

questions.prompt:
Read the poem.

The storm crashed and boomed.
Dark clouds rolled by.
Cold rain fell down.

Which words help you HEAR the storm?
questions.hint: Hearing words are sounds. Say each choice out loud and ask which one is a noise your ears would catch.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! 'Crashed and boomed' are loud sound words you can hear. Nice work!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Almost! Which words make a loud sound your ears can hear? Read the storm words again.
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! 'Crashed and boomed' help you hear the storm. Now you know!

questions.prompt:
The old gate creaked open.
"I missed this place!" Lily said, hugging the fence.

Which words best show that Lily is HAPPY to be back?
choices: 'I missed this place!' and hugging the fence
questions.correct: 'I missed this place!' and hugging the fence
questions.hint: A feeling is shown by what a person says and does. A sound word alone does not tell you a feeling.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! Lily said she missed this place and hugged the fence. That shows she is happy! Nice work!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Close one! Which words show Lily is glad to be back? Look at what she said and did.
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! 'I missed this place!' and hugging the fence show she is happy. Now you know!

questions.prompt:
Read the poem.

Nana's kitchen smelled like warm cookies.
The tile felt cold.
A timer beeped.

Which words appeal to your sense of SMELL?
questions.hint: Smell words tell what your nose notices. Which line could you smell, not hear or touch?
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! 'Smelled like warm cookies' is something your nose can smell. Nice work!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Take another look! Your nose smells things. Which words tell about a smell in the kitchen?
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! 'Smelled like warm cookies' appeals to smell. Now you know!

standards.standard_description: Explain major differences between books that tell stories and books that give information
standards.parent_tip: When picking books, ask: Is this a made-up story or does it teach us real facts?

questions.prompt: Listen to the story: Mia read a storybook. It had bright pictures and was very long. It was about a talking dragon that flew to the moon, all made up and not real. Which is TRUE about storybooks?
choices: They have made-up characters and events
questions.correct: They have made-up characters and events
questions.hint: Stories come from someone's imagination.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! A storybook can have a talking dragon, so it has made-up characters and events. Nice work!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Hmm, think about the talking dragon that flew to the moon. Is that real or made up?
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! Storybooks have made-up characters and events. Now you know!

questions.prompt: Listen to the story: Sam read an informational book about real sharks. It taught true facts, like how sharks swim and eat. Nothing in it was make-believe, and it was not silly or funny. Which is TRUE about informational books?
questions.hint: Informational means it gives you real information.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! Sam's book taught true things about real sharks. It teaches real facts! Nice work!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Good try! Sam's book was about real sharks and nothing make-believe. What do those books do?
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! Informational books teach real facts. Now you know!

questions.prompt: Here is the start of a book: "Turtle was slow, but he never gave up. He wanted to win the big race more than anything." What kind of book is this most likely to be?
choices: A true book about a real turtle
A book of real turtle facts
A book that teaches how turtles swim
questions.hint: Look for clues about the turtle. Does he have feelings and a wish, or does the book just teach real facts?
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! A turtle who wants to win a race is make-believe. It is a story! Nice work!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Almost! Turtles cannot really wish to win a race. Is this book real or make-believe?
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! This is most likely a make-believe story. Now you know!

questions.prompt: Two books are about rain. One book tells the true way that clouds make rain. The other is about a raindrop named Dot who is afraid to fall. Which book gives you information?
choices: The book that is more fun to read
The book about how clouds make rain
The book about Dot the raindrop
Both books, because both are about rain
questions.correct: The book about how clouds make rain
questions.hint: Both books share the same topic. Ask which one teaches real, true facts and which one tells a made-up tale.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! The book that tells the true way clouds make rain gives you real information. Nice work!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Close one! One book is a true way, one is about a scared raindrop. Which one teaches real facts?
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! The book about how clouds make rain gives information. Now you know!

questions.prompt: Lin's book has a girl who shows you how seeds really grow into plants. Everything the book teaches is true. Is this book more like a story or an information book?
choices: A story, because stories are always fun
A make-believe tale about a magic seed
An information book, because it teaches true things
A make-believe story, because it has a girl in it
questions.correct: An information book, because it teaches true things
questions.hint: A book can have a person in it and still teach real facts. Decide by asking: is the book teaching true things, or telling a made-up tale?
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! Lin's book teaches the true way seeds grow, so it is an information book. Nice work!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Take another look! Everything in Lin's book is true. Does that make it a story or a teaching book?
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! It is an information book, because it teaches true things. Now you know!

questions.prompt: Maya wants to find out true facts about how spiders spin their webs. Which book should she choose?
choices: A funny book about a spider's silly dream
A story about a spider who is late for school
A tale about a spider who cannot find her shoes
A book that explains how spiders really spin webs
questions.correct: A book that explains how spiders really spin webs
questions.hint: Every choice is about spiders. Match Maya's goal — learning true facts — to the right kind of book.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! Maya wants true facts, so she needs the book that tells how spiders really spin webs. Nice work!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Hmm, think again. Maya wants TRUE facts, not a silly or made-up tale. Which book teaches real things?
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! Maya should pick the book that explains how spiders really spin webs, because it has true facts. Now you know!

questions.prompt: Listen to the story: Real seeds cannot talk or yawn like people. In real life, a seed just needs water and sun to grow. Only in a make-believe story can a seed do things like a person. Which sentence sounds like it is from a make-believe story?
choices: The little seed yawned and asked the sun to wake it up
A seed needs water and sun to grow
questions.correct: The little seed yawned and asked the sun to wake it up
questions.hint: Three of these tell real facts about seeds. Find the one where the seed does something a real seed could never do.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! A real seed cannot yawn or talk, so that sentence must be make-believe. Nice work!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Take another look. In real life, seeds just grow. Which sentence shows a seed doing something a real seed could never do?
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! The make-believe line is the one where the little seed yawned and asked the sun to wake it up. Real seeds cannot do that! Now you know!

questions.prompt: A book is about a dragon who can talk and fly to the moon. Is this book a story or does it give real facts?
questions.hint: Real dragons that talk and fly to the moon are not real. That means it is a story someone made up.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! A dragon that talks and flies to the moon is not real, so this is a made-up story. Nice work!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Almost! A talking, flying dragon is not real. So is this book about true facts, or is it pretend?
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! It is a made-up story, because a real dragon cannot talk or fly to the moon. Now you know!

questions.prompt: A book teaches you real facts about how cows give us milk. What kind of book is this?
questions.hint: When a book teaches you real, true facts, it is an information book.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! This book teaches real facts about cows and milk, so it is an information book. Nice work!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Good try! This book teaches you real, true facts. What do we call a book that gives us facts?
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! It is an information book, because it teaches you true facts about how cows give us milk. Now you know!

questions.prompt: Listen to the story: A story book has make-believe characters who do things, like a bear who goes to school with his friends. A fact book does not tell a tale; it just shows real things, like a list of animal names or real bug photos. Which book tells a STORY?
choices: A book of real bug photos
A book that shows facts about real bears
A book about a bear who goes to school with his friends
questions.correct: A book about a bear who goes to school with his friends
questions.hint: A bear who goes to school with friends is not real. A story has made-up characters like that.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! A bear who goes to school is make-believe, so that book tells a story. Nice work!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Close one! A story book has make-believe characters doing things. Which choice shows a pretend character, not just real facts?
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! The story is the book about a bear who goes to school with his friends, because that bear is make-believe. Now you know!

questions.prompt: Listen to the story: An information book gives you true facts about real things, like the sun. Make-believe books tell tales that are not real, like a magic hat, a flying cat, or a talking fish. Which book gives real INFORMATION?
choices: A book that tells you true facts about the sun
A tale about a magic hat
A story about a flying cat
A book about a fish who talks
questions.correct: A book that tells you true facts about the sun
questions.hint: True facts about something real, like the sun, means the book gives information.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! Facts about the real sun are true, so that book gives real information. Nice work!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Not yet! A magic hat, flying cat, and talking fish are all pretend. Which book is about something real?
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! The book that tells you true facts about the sun gives real information, because the sun is real. Now you know!

questions.prompt: A book is full of true facts and real photos about trucks. Does it tell a story or give information?
questions.hint: True facts and real photos are the clue. A book like that gives you information.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! True facts and real photos of trucks mean this book gives information. Nice work!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Hmm, look again. This book has true facts and real photos. Does that tell a pretend tale, or share real facts?
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! This book gives information, because it is full of true facts and real photos about trucks. Now you know!

standards.standard_description: Identify who is telling the story at various points in a text
standards.parent_tip: Ask your child: Who is talking right now in the story? Is it a character or someone outside the story?

questions.prompt:
I went to the store with my mom. I picked out a red apple.

Who is telling this story?
questions.hint: Look at the word 'I' — who is the 'I' in the story?
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! The word "I" means the child is telling it about their own trip. Nice work!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Almost! Look at the word "I" in the story. Who says "I" when they talk about themselves?
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! The child, the one who says "I," is telling this story. Now you know!

questions.prompt:
Sam ran to the park. He played on the swings.

Who is telling this story?
questions.hint: The story says 'He' not 'I' — someone else is telling us about Sam.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! It says "Sam" and "He," not "I," so someone outside the story tells it. Nice work!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Good try! The story says "Sam" and "he," never "I." So is Sam telling it, or is someone else?
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! A narrator outside the story tells it, because it says "Sam" and "he," not "I." Now you know!

questions.prompt: Who is telling you that Maria said, "I love pizza!"?
questions.hint: Think about who is telling the whole story, including what the characters say.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! Maria's words are in quotes, but someone else tells us she said them. That is the narrator. Nice work!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Take another look. Maria spoke the words, but who is telling YOU that she said them?
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! The narrator tells you that Maria said it. Maria spoke, but the narrator shares her words. Now you know!

questions.prompt: In the sentence, ""Let's go!" shouted Tom," who is telling us that Tom shouted?
questions.hint: Look at the words that are not inside the quotation marks. Who usually says those words to help us understand what is happening?
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! Tom said the words, but someone else tells us that he shouted. That is the storyteller. Nice work!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Close one! Tom did the shouting, but who is telling US that Tom shouted?
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! The person telling the story tells us that Tom shouted. Now you know!

questions.prompt:
Mia and her cat sat by the window. "I see a bird!" Mia said. The cat did not move.

Who is telling this story?
choices: A narrator who is not in the story
questions.correct: A narrator who is not in the story
questions.hint: The word "I" only shows up inside the quote marks. Ask who is telling the WHOLE story, not just who says one line out loud.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! It says "Mia" and "the cat," not "I," so someone outside is telling it. Nice work!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Hmm, look again. The story never says "I." It names Mia and the cat. So who is telling it?
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! A narrator who is not in the story tells it, because it names Mia and the cat instead of saying "I." Now you know!

questions.prompt:
I love bedtime. Every night my dad reads to me. Tonight he read, "The frog jumped over the log."

Who is telling this whole story?
choices: The kid whose dad reads to them
questions.correct: The kid whose dad reads to them
questions.hint: There is a little story hidden inside the big story. Find who is telling the BIG story that starts with "I love bedtime."
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! The word "me" and "my dad" tell us the kid is sharing their own bedtime. Nice work!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Not yet! Find the words "my dad" and "reads to me." Who is the person saying "me"?
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! The kid whose dad reads to them is telling the story, because they say "my dad" and "me." Now you know!

questions.prompt:
Ben told his sister about his day. "I fell down and I got up. I ran the race and I won!"

In the part inside the quote marks, who is talking?
questions.hint: The first sentence is told by a narrator. But the words inside the quote marks are said out loud by one person. Who is that?
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! The words inside the quote marks are what he said, so that is Ben talking. Nice work!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Good try! The quote marks show the exact words someone spoke out loud. Who said "I fell down"?
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! Ben is talking inside the quote marks, because those are the words he said. Now you know!

questions.prompt:
Grandma got a note in the mail. It said, "I miss you. Please come visit soon. Love, Sam."

Who is telling the part inside the note?
questions.hint: Someone wrote the note, and they signed their name at the end. Look for who says "Love, ___."
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! The note says "Love, Sam," so the words inside the note come from Sam. Nice work!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Almost! Look at who signed the note at the very end. Whose words are inside the note?
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! Sam is telling the part inside the note, because the note says "Love, Sam." Now you know!

questions.prompt:
My name is Rosa. I have a dog named Max. Max cannot talk, but if he could, he would say, "I want a bone!"

Who is telling most of this story?
choices: A narrator not in the story
questions.hint: There are two "I" voices here. One is only pretend. Find who is telling almost all of the story from start to end.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! She says "My name is Rosa" and "I," so Rosa is telling most of it. Nice work!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Take another look. Most of the story uses "I" and "my." Who says "My name is..." at the start?
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! Rosa tells most of the story, because she says "My name is Rosa" and uses "I." Now you know!

standards.standard_description: Use illustrations and details in a story to describe its characters, setting, or events
standards.parent_tip: Point to pictures in books and ask: What does this picture tell us that the words don't?

questions.prompt: If a picture shows a girl with a big smile and a trophy, how does the girl probably feel?
questions.hint: What do smiles and trophies usually mean?
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! A big smile and a trophy show she won, so she feels proud and happy. Nice work!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Hmm, think again. She has a big SMILE and holds a TROPHY. Do those show a good feeling or a bad one?
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! The girl feels proud and happy, because she is smiling and holding a trophy she won. Now you know!

questions.prompt: A picture shows snow on the ground and kids wearing coats. What season is it?
questions.hint: Snow and coats are clues about the season.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! Snow on the ground and warm coats tell us it is winter. Nice work!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Almost! The picture shows SNOW and kids in warm coats. Which cold season has snow?
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! It is winter, because there is snow on the ground and the kids wear coats. Now you know!

questions.prompt: The story says, "The dog was big." Look at the picture that goes with the story. What does the picture help you understand about how big the dog is?
choices: The dog is small enough to fit in a basket.
The dog is about the same height as the boy.
The dog is sleeping on the floor.
The dog is much smaller than the boy.
questions.correct: The dog is about the same height as the boy.
questions.hint: Look closely at the dog and the boy in the picture. How do their sizes compare?
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! The picture shows the dog reaching up as tall as the boy, so it is a big dog! Nice work!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Close one! The word "big" is a clue. In the picture, how does the dog's size compare to the boy?
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! The dog is about the same height as the boy, which shows us it is a big dog. Now you know!

questions.prompt: Imagine a picture in the story shows a bunny with a big smile, a small backpack, and eyes looking towards a tall, snowy mountain. Using this picture, what can we best describe about the bunny?
choices: The bunny is tired and wants to rest.
The bunny is hungry for a snack.
The bunny is excited to go on a trip.
The bunny is afraid of the mountain.
questions.correct: The bunny is excited to go on a trip.
questions.hint: Look at the bunny's smile, backpack, and where its eyes are pointing in the picture to understand its feelings or plans.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! A smile, a packed backpack, and eyes on the mountain show the bunny is excited for a trip! Nice work!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Take another look. The bunny SMILES, has a BACKPACK, and looks at the mountain. What is the bunny getting ready to do?
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! The bunny is excited to go on a trip. The smile, backpack, and mountain are all clues. Now you know!

questions.prompt: A picture shows a boy with tears on his face standing next to a broken toy. How does the boy feel?
questions.hint: Tears and a broken toy — what feeling does that show?
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! Tears and a broken toy show the boy feels sad. Nice work!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Not yet! The boy has TEARS and his toy is broken. What feeling do tears usually show?
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! The boy feels sad, because he has tears and his toy is broken. Now you know!

questions.prompt: The picture shows Mia in a coat and a warm hat. The trees behind her have no leaves at all. On the ground, kids are jumping into a big pile of red and orange leaves. When does this story most likely happen?
questions.hint: Some clues fit more than one season. Find the one clue that fits only ONE season.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! Bare trees and kids jumping in red and orange leaves tell us it is fall. Nice work!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Hmm, look at the clues. There is NO snow, but the leaves are red and orange and dropping down. Which season is that?
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! It is fall, because the leaves are red and orange and dropping, with no snow yet. Now you know!

questions.prompt: Sam is holding his shiny new red bike and smiling. But his hands are up near his face, and he is peeking through his fingers at the tall, steep hill in front of him. How does Sam most likely feel?
questions.hint: Look at BOTH what his mouth is doing and what his hands are doing. They tell different feelings.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! A smile shows Sam is happy, but peeking through his fingers shows he is a little scared too. Nice work!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Good try! Sam is SMILING but also peeking through his fingers at a steep hill. That shows TWO feelings mixed. Which one?
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! Sam feels excited but a little scared. He smiles, yet he peeks nervously at the big hill. Now you know!

questions.prompt: The picture shows Ben sitting on the floor. Blocks are scattered all around him. A tall block tower lies on its side, broken into pieces. Ben's mouth is open wide. What most likely happened just BEFORE this picture?
choices: Ben put all his blocks away
Ben found the blocks inside a box
Ben knocked over his block tower
Ben is just starting to build a tower
questions.correct: Ben knocked over his block tower
questions.hint: The tower is already broken and the blocks are spread out. Think about what had to happen first to make it look this way.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! Blocks scattered and a broken tower show Ben just knocked it over. Nice work!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Take another look. The tower is broken and blocks are everywhere, and his mouth is open wide. What happened to that tower?
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! Ben knocked over his block tower. That is why the blocks are scattered and the tower fell. Now you know!

questions.prompt: At the lunch table, Tom's plate is empty and he is rubbing his belly with a big smile. Right next to him, Kim's plate is still full of food, and she is looking away with her arms crossed. Who was probably hungry?
questions.hint: Compare the two plates and what each child's body is doing. Who used up their food?
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! An empty plate and a rubbed belly show this one was hungry and ate it all up. Nice work!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Close one! One kid has an EMPTY plate and rubs his belly. The other has a FULL plate. Who ate up all their food?
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! Tom was hungry. His plate is empty and he rubs his belly with a smile. Now you know!

questions.prompt: The picture shows a dog hiding under a table. Its ears are down and its whole body is shaking. Out the window behind the dog, the sky is dark gray and a bright light flashes. What is the dog most likely afraid of?
questions.hint: The reason isn't in the room with the dog. Look out the window for the clue.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! A dark sky and a flash of light mean a storm, and the shaking dog is afraid of it. Nice work!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Not yet! The dog is shaking and hiding. Look out the window at the dark sky and bright flash. What is out there?
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! The dog is afraid of the storm outside. The dark sky and bright flash are the clues. Now you know!

standards.standard_description: Compare and contrast the adventures and experiences of characters in stories
standards.parent_tip: After reading two stories, ask: How are these characters the same? How are they different?

questions.prompt: Listen to the story: In one story, a hungry bear looks all around the woods to find honey to eat. In another story, a busy bee flies from flower to flower and works hard to make honey. What is different about how the bear and the bee experience honey in their stories?
choices: The bear enjoys eating honey, and the bee enjoys flying near it.
Both the bear and the bee collect honey for their families.
The bear wants to find honey, while the bee works to make honey.
The bear shares honey, and the bee hides honey.
questions.correct: The bear wants to find honey, while the bee works to make honey.
questions.hint: Look closely at what each character does with honey in their own story.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! The bear looks for honey, but the bee makes it. That is the big difference! Nice work!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Hmm, think again. The bear searches for honey to eat. The bee flies and works hard. Does the bee FIND honey or MAKE it?
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! The bear wants to find honey, while the bee works to make honey. Now you know!

questions.prompt:
Story 1: Amy is brave and climbs a mountain. Story 2: Bob is brave and swims across a lake.

How are Amy and Bob alike?
questions.hint: What trait do both characters share?
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! Amy climbs and Bob swims, but both do a bold thing, so both are brave! Nice work!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Almost! Amy climbs a mountain and Bob swims a lake. Both do something bold. What word tells how they both are?
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! Amy and Bob are both brave. They each do a hard, bold thing. Now you know!

questions.prompt:
Story 1: The cat stays inside all day. Story 2: The dog plays outside all day.

How are the cat and dog DIFFERENT?
choices: One is big, one is small
One stays inside, one goes outside
questions.correct: One stays inside, one goes outside
questions.hint: Where does each animal spend the day?
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! The cat stays in and the dog goes out, so that is how they are different. Nice work!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Good try! The cat stays IN the house. The dog plays OUT in the yard. How are those two places different?
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! The difference is that one stays inside and one goes outside. Now you know!

questions.prompt: Leo went to the park and played on the swings. His friend Mia went to the playground and played on the slide. How were Leo's and Mia's adventures the same?
choices: They both played inside a house.
questions.hint: Look at where Leo went and where Mia went to find what was the same about their adventures.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! A park and a playground are both outside, so both kids played outdoors. Nice work!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Close one! Leo was at a park and Mia was at a playground. Are those places inside a house, or out in the open?
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! Leo and Mia both played outside. A park and a playground are both outdoors. Now you know!

questions.prompt: In our story, a little bird named Pip loves to fly high in the sky. A little rabbit named Fluffy loves to dig tunnels deep in the ground. What is one way Pip and Fluffy are *different*?
choices: Pip likes to fly, and Fluffy likes to dig.
Pip is small, and Fluffy is big.
questions.correct: Pip likes to fly, and Fluffy likes to dig.
questions.hint: Think about what each character likes to do. How are their favorite activities not the same?
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! Pip flies up high and Fluffy digs down low, so they like different things. Nice work!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Take another look. Pip loves to fly high. Fluffy loves to dig deep. Those are two very different things to do!
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! Pip likes to fly, and Fluffy likes to dig. That is one way they are different. Now you know!

questions.prompt:
Story 1: Mia lost her red ball. She looked for it all day. At night she found it under her bed and hugged it.
Story 2: Sam lost his kite. He looked for a little while, then got a new toy and played happily.

How are Mia and Sam DIFFERENT in how they felt about losing their toy?
choices: Mia cared a lot about her ball, but Sam did not mind losing his kite.
Both Mia and Sam looked all day and never stopped.
Both Mia and Sam were happy to lose their toys.
Mia got a new toy, and Sam found his old one under the bed.
questions.correct: Mia cared a lot about her ball, but Sam did not mind losing his kite.
questions.hint: The stories never say 'cared' or 'did not mind.' Look at what each child DID — how long they searched and how they acted — to figure out their feelings.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! Mia searched all day for her ball, but Sam happily got a new toy. Their feelings were different! Nice work!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Hmm, think again. Mia looked ALL day and hugged her ball. Sam quickly got a new toy and played. Who cared more?
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! Mia cared a lot about her ball, but Sam did not mind losing his kite. Now you know!

questions.prompt:
Story 1: Rosa moved to a new town. At lunch she waved to a girl, and they ate together.
Story 2: Ben moved to a new town. At lunch he sat by himself and looked at the floor.

How did Rosa and Ben feel DIFFERENTLY on their first day?
choices: Both Rosa and Ben made a new friend.
Rosa felt friendly, but Ben felt lonely.
Both Rosa and Ben felt lonely.
Rosa felt scared, but Ben felt brave.
questions.correct: Rosa felt friendly, but Ben felt lonely.
questions.hint: Neither story uses a feeling word. Picture what each child does at lunch and decide which feeling matches each action.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! Rosa waved and made a friend, but Ben sat alone. They felt very different. Nice work!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Not yet! Rosa WAVED and ate with a girl. Ben sat BY HIMSELF and looked down. One felt warm, one felt alone. Which is which?
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! Rosa felt friendly, but Ben felt lonely. Now you know!

questions.prompt:
Story 1: A little ant carried a crumb up a big hill. It was tired but did not stop.
Story 2: A little mouse carried a nut up a big hill. Halfway up, it dropped the nut and went home.

How were the ant and the mouse DIFFERENT?
choices: The ant dropped its food, but the mouse kept going.
Both the ant and the mouse reached the top.
The ant kept going, but the mouse gave up.
Both the ant and the mouse gave up.
questions.correct: The ant kept going, but the mouse gave up.
questions.hint: Follow each animal all the way to the end of its story. Who finished the climb and who stopped early?
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! The ant was tired but did not stop, while the mouse quit halfway. That is the difference! Nice work!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Good try! The ant was tired but did NOT stop. The mouse dropped the nut and went home. Who kept going and who quit?
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! The ant kept going, but the mouse gave up. Now you know!

questions.prompt:
Story 1: It started to rain. Lily jumped in the puddles and laughed.
Story 2: It started to rain. Max ran inside and shut the door.

How did Lily and Max feel about the rain DIFFERENTLY?
choices: Both Lily and Max were sad about the rain.
Both Lily and Max loved the rain.
Lily ran inside, and Max jumped in the puddles.
Lily was glad it rained, but Max wanted to get away from it.
questions.correct: Lily was glad it rained, but Max wanted to get away from it.
questions.hint: The same thing happened to both kids — it rained. Watch what each one chose to do to know how they felt about it.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! Lily jumped and laughed in the rain, but Max ran inside. They felt the opposite! Nice work!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Close one! Lily jumped in puddles and LAUGHED. Max ran inside and SHUT the door. Who liked the rain and who did not?
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! Lily was glad it rained, but Max wanted to get away from it. Now you know!

questions.prompt:
Story 1: Tom wanted a pet. He fed a stray cat every day. Soon the cat followed him home.
Story 2: Kate wanted a pet. She saw a stray cat but never went near it. The cat ran away.

Both Tom and Kate wanted a pet. Why did Tom get a cat but Kate did not?
choices: Tom fed the cat every day, so it learned to trust him and came home.
Tom already had a pet cat waiting at home.
Kate chased the cat away on purpose.
Kate fed the cat too, but it still ran off.
questions.correct: Tom fed the cat every day, so it learned to trust him and came home.
questions.hint: Compare what Tom did with the cat to what Kate did. Connect his action to why the cat chose to follow him.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! Tom fed the cat every day, so it trusted him. Kate stayed away, so hers ran off. Nice work!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Take another look. Tom fed the cat EVERY day. Kate never went near hers. Why would a cat come home to Tom?
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! Tom fed the cat every day, so it learned to trust him and came home. Now you know!

standards.standard_description: Ask and answer questions about key details in an informational text
standards.parent_tip: When reading nonfiction, pause and ask: What did we just learn? What's the most important fact?

questions.prompt: Based on the text, what kind of weather do penguins like?
questions.hint: Look at the first sentence to find out where penguins live.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! The text tells us penguins like cold places, so snowy and icy weather is best. Nice work!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Hmm, think about where penguins live. Do they like it hot and sunny, or cold and icy?
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! Penguins like snowy and icy weather, because they live in very cold places. Now you know!

questions.prompt:
Bees make honey. They live together in a hive. A queen bee leads the group.

Who leads the bees?
questions.hint: The last sentence tells you who leads.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! The text says a queen bee leads the group. You found the fact! Nice work!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Almost! Read the last line again. It tells who leads the group of bees. What kind of bee is that?
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! A queen bee leads the bees. The text says the queen bee leads the group. Now you know!

questions.prompt:
The sun is a star. It gives Earth light and heat. Without the sun, Earth would be very cold.

What does the sun give Earth?
questions.hint: Read the second sentence carefully.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! The text says the sun gives Earth light and heat. Great finding! Nice work!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Good try! Read the line about the sun again. What two things does it say the sun gives to Earth?
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! The sun gives Earth light and heat. Without it, Earth would be cold. Now you know!

questions.prompt:
Dogs need fresh water every day. They also need food and exercise to stay healthy.

What do dogs need to stay healthy?
questions.hint: Both sentences tell you what dogs need.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! The text lists all three: water, food, and exercise. You caught them all! Nice work!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Close one! The text names more than one thing dogs need. Try to find ALL of them, not just one.
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! Dogs need water, food, and exercise to stay healthy. The text names all three. Now you know!

questions.prompt:
Frogs start as eggs in the water. Then they become tadpoles. Finally, they grow into frogs.

What comes AFTER the egg stage?
questions.hint: Look at the order: eggs, then what?
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! First eggs, then tadpoles, then frogs. Tadpole comes right after the egg. Nice work!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Take another look at the order. First comes the egg. What is the very next step before it becomes a frog?
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! After the egg comes the tadpole. Then the tadpole grows into a frog. Now you know!

questions.prompt:
Camels live in the hot desert. There is very little water in the desert. A camel can go many days without a drink.

Why is it helpful for a camel to go many days without water?
choices: Because the desert has very little water
Because camels drink only at night
Because the desert is very hot
Because camels do not like water
questions.correct: Because the desert has very little water
questions.hint: Put two facts together: what is hard to find in the desert, and what a camel can do without.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! The desert has very little water, so going without a drink helps the camel live there. Nice work!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Hmm, think about the desert. The text says there is very little water there. Why would that skill help a camel?
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! It helps because the desert has very little water, so the camel can go days without a drink. Now you know!

questions.prompt:
Bats sleep all day long. They wake up when the sky gets dark. After they wake up, bats fly around and catch bugs to eat.

When do bats look for food?
choices: In the middle of the day
questions.hint: Find when bats wake up, then find when they eat. Both clues point to the same time.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! Bats wake up when the sky gets dark and fly out to catch bugs. That means night! Nice work!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Almost! The text says bats sleep all day and wake up when the sky gets dark. What time is dark out?
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! Bats look for food at night, when they wake up and catch bugs. Now you know!

questions.prompt:
A turtle carries a hard shell on its back. When a turtle is scared, it pulls its head and legs inside the shell. The shell keeps the turtle safe.

What will a turtle most likely do when a hungry fox comes close?
questions.hint: A fox coming close is scary. What does the text say a turtle does when it feels scared?
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! When a turtle is scared, it pulls into its shell to stay safe. Nice work!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Good try! The text says a scared turtle uses its shell to stay safe. What would it do when a fox comes close?
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! The turtle will hide inside its shell, because that is how it stays safe when scared. Now you know!

questions.prompt:
Maple trees have green leaves in summer. In fall, the leaves turn red and orange. Then the leaves drop off. In winter, the tree has no leaves at all.

What color are the maple leaves in the season right before winter?
questions.hint: First figure out which season comes right before winter, then find that season's leaf color.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! The season right before winter is fall, when maple leaves turn red and orange. Nice work!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Take another look. Winter has no leaves. What color are the leaves in the season JUST before winter?
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! Right before winter, in fall, the maple leaves are red and orange. Now you know!

questions.prompt:
Firefighters wear thick coats to their job. Their coats are made so they do not burn easily. Firefighters get very close to fire when they work.

Why do firefighters wear coats that do not burn easily?
choices: To keep safe when they are close to fire
To look the same as each other
To stay warm on cold days
To run faster to the fire truck
questions.correct: To keep safe when they are close to fire
questions.hint: Think about where firefighters stand when they work, and what a coat that won't burn is good for there.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! Firefighters get close to fire, so their coats keep them safe. Nice work!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Hmm, think about where firefighters work. What are they very close to? That is the big clue.
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! They wear those coats to keep safe when they are close to fire. Now you know!

standards.standard_description: Identify the main topic and retell key details of a text
standards.parent_tip: Ask your child: What is this book mostly about? Can you tell me three things you learned?

questions.prompt:
Apples can be red, green, or yellow. They grow on trees. Apples are a healthy snack.

What is the main topic?
questions.hint: Every sentence is about the same thing — what is it?
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! Every sentence tells us about apples, so that is the main topic. Nice work!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Almost! What one thing does every sentence talk about? Read them again and see.
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! The main topic is apples, because the whole text is about them. Now you know!

questions.prompt:
Firefighters wear special gear. They drive fire trucks. They put out fires and help people.

What is the main topic?
questions.hint: What is every sentence about?
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! Every sentence tells about firefighters, so they are the main topic. Nice work!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Good try! Who wears the gear, drives the truck, and puts out fires? That is your answer.
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! The main topic is firefighters, since the text is all about them. Now you know!

questions.prompt: Listen to the story: A short book tells us just one fact about cats. It says that cats like to sleep a lot, sometimes all day. The book says nothing about their tails, chasing mice, or climbing trees. Which detail about cats is shared in the text?
choices: Cats like to sleep a lot.
questions.correct: Cats like to sleep a lot.
questions.hint: The passage tells you one thing that cats like to do very much.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! The book says cats sleep a lot, so that is the detail we know. Nice work!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Close one! The book only told us ONE fact about cats. What did it say they do a lot?
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! The text tells us cats like to sleep a lot. The rest was not in the book. Now you know!

questions.prompt:
The ocean is full of salt water. Many animals live in the ocean. Whales, fish, and dolphins swim there.

What is the main topic?
questions.hint: All the sentences tell us about the same place.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! Salt water and all those animals live in the ocean, so that is the main topic. Nice work!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Not yet! Where does the salt water and all the animals belong? That big place is the topic.
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! The main topic is the ocean, the big place where it all happens. Now you know!

questions.prompt:
Teeth are important. You should brush them twice a day. Eating too much candy can hurt your teeth.

Which is a key detail?
questions.hint: Read the second sentence for a key detail.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! The text says to brush your teeth twice a day. That is a key detail. Nice work!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Take another look! How many times each day should you brush? The text tells you.
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! A key detail is to brush your teeth twice a day. Now you know!

questions.prompt:
Bees live in a hive. They buzz from flower to flower. Bees make sweet honey for us to eat. One hive can hold many bees.

What is this text MOSTLY about?
questions.hint: Look for the one thing that every sentence talks about, not just a word you see one time.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! The hive, flowers, and honey all connect to bees. Bees are what it is mostly about. Nice work!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Hmm, the hive, flowers, and honey are only parts. Who does the whole text follow? Think again.
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! The text is mostly about bees. Everything else connects back to them. Now you know!

questions.prompt:
It has a soft mane. It runs fast across the grass. Children love to ride on its back. It says 'neigh, neigh!'

What is this text MOSTLY about?
questions.hint: Add up all the clues about the animal, then think of which animal fits every one.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! A mane, running fast, and 'neigh' all point to a horse. Nice work!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Almost! What animal has a mane, runs on grass, and says 'neigh'? Put the clues together.
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! The clues all point to a horse. Now you know!

questions.prompt:
Rain helps plants grow big. It fills up rivers and lakes. Animals drink the water when they are thirsty. Rain is good for the whole earth.

Which sentence tells the BIG main idea?
choices: Rain is good for the whole earth.
It fills up rivers and lakes.
questions.correct: Rain is good for the whole earth.
questions.hint: The main idea is the big one that all the other sentences help explain.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! That sentence covers plants, rivers, and animals all at once. It is the big idea. Nice work!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Good try! One sentence covers ALL the others, not just one part. Which one is the biggest?
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! The big main idea is that rain is good for the whole earth. It sums up all the rest. Now you know!

questions.prompt:
Some ants dig tunnels under the ground. Other ants build nests up in trees. Ants work together to carry food. Ants are very strong for their small size.

What would be the BEST title for this text?
questions.hint: A good title is not too small and not too big — it should cover the whole text.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! The text tells lots of things about ants, so that title fits best. Nice work!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Close one! The best title covers EVERYTHING about ants, not just one part. Which one is the biggest?
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! The best title is All About Ants, because it covers the whole text. Now you know!

questions.prompt:
Sam's little plant looked sad. Its leaves were turning brown. Sam gave the plant water every single day. Soon the leaves were green again!

Why did the leaves turn green?
questions.hint: Find what Sam did right before the leaves changed, even though the text never says 'because.'
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! Sam watered the plant every day, and then the leaves turned green. Nice work!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Hmm, what did Sam do every single day right before the leaves turned green? That is the cause.
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! The leaves turned green because Sam gave it water. Now you know!

standards.standard_description: Describe the connection between two individuals, events, ideas, or pieces of information in a text
standards.parent_tip: Help your child see connections: 'Because it rained, the flowers grew. See how one thing leads to another?'

questions.prompt:
It rained all day. The next morning, puddles were everywhere.

Why were there puddles?
questions.hint: What happened first that caused the puddles?
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! It rained all day, so the water made puddles. Nice work!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Not yet! What happened all day long before the puddles showed up? That is why they are there.
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! There were puddles because it rained all day. Now you know!

questions.prompt:
Seeds need water and sunlight to grow. A farmer waters the seeds every day. Soon, small plants appear.

Why do small plants appear?
questions.hint: What does the farmer do that helps the seeds?
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! The farmer waters the seeds every day, so the plants grow. Nice work!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Take another look! What does the farmer do every day to help the seeds? That makes the plants grow.
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! The plants appear because the farmer waters them. Now you know!

questions.prompt:
Bears eat a lot in the fall. In winter, they hibernate and sleep for months.

Why do bears eat a lot in fall?
choices: To get ready for winter sleep
questions.correct: To get ready for winter sleep
questions.hint: What happens AFTER they eat a lot?
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! Bears eat a lot in fall to get ready for their long winter sleep. Nice work!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Hmm, what do bears do all winter? They eat a lot in fall to get ready for that. Think again.
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! Bears eat a lot to get ready for winter sleep. Now you know!

questions.prompt: Listen to the story: The book tells about George Washington. First, he led the army in a big war and helped win it. After the war was over, people chose him to be the first president. How does the text show the connection between George Washington leading the army and being president?
choices: He led the army after he was president.
He led the army before he was president.
He led the army at the same time he was president.
The text does not tell us how they are connected.
questions.correct: He led the army before he was president.
questions.hint: Look for the word in the text that tells you when he led the army compared to being president.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! First he led the army, then people chose him as president. Nice work!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Almost! The word 'first' is a clue. Which did he do first, and which came after? Read again.
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! He led the army before he was president. Now you know!

questions.prompt: The passage tells us about the caterpillar's big change. What does the caterpillar do right after eating to get ready for this change?
questions.hint: Read what the caterpillar does immediately after eating leaves, which is also before it becomes a butterfly.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! Right after eating, the caterpillar makes a cocoon to get ready. Nice work!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Good try! What does the caterpillar build to get ready for its big change? Think about that step.
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! Right after eating, it makes a cocoon. Now you know!

questions.prompt:
Mia's dog was in the yard. Her baby brother liked to play by the gate. Later, the gate was open and the dog was gone.

Who most likely opened the gate?
questions.hint: Look for who was near the gate before it was found open. Match that person to the event.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! The baby brother liked to play by the gate, so he most likely opened it. Nice work!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Close one! Who liked to play right by the gate? That person most likely opened it. Look again.
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! Her baby brother most likely opened the gate, since he played there. Now you know!

questions.prompt:
Sam shares his crayons with Tom every day. Today Sam had no crayons of his own. Tom gave Sam some of his crayons.

Why did Tom give Sam some crayons?
choices: Sam had always shared with him
The teacher made him do it
questions.correct: Sam had always shared with him
questions.hint: Think about what Sam did for Tom on the other days, and how that might make Tom act back.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! Sam always shared his crayons, so Tom shared back with him. Nice work!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Hmm, what did Sam do for Tom every day? Tom did the same kind thing back. Think again.
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! Tom gave Sam crayons because Sam had always shared with him. Now you know!

questions.prompt:
In fall, the leaves turn colors and drop to the ground. People rake the leaves into big piles. By winter, the trees are bare with no leaves at all.

Why are the trees bare in winter?
choices: The leaves fell off in fall
People raked them off the trees
questions.correct: The leaves fell off in fall
questions.hint: Find the sentence that tells what happened to the leaves before winter came. Connect that event to the bare trees.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! The leaves dropped off in fall, so by winter the trees were bare. Nice work!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Not yet! What happened to the leaves back in fall? That is why the trees are bare now. Reread it.
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! The trees are bare because the leaves fell off in fall. Now you know!

questions.prompt:
Turtles carry their shells on their backs. When danger comes, a turtle hides inside its shell. A rabbit has no shell, so when danger comes it runs to its hole.

Why does the rabbit run to its hole instead of hiding like the turtle?
choices: Its hole is warm and soft
It is faster than a turtle
It is more scared than a turtle
It has no shell to hide in
questions.correct: It has no shell to hide in
questions.hint: Compare the two animals. What does the turtle have that the rabbit does not, and how does that change what each one does?
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! A turtle hides in its shell, but a rabbit has none, so it runs. Nice work!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Take another look! The turtle hides in something the rabbit does not have. What is that? That is why it runs.
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! The rabbit runs because it has no shell to hide in. Now you know!

questions.prompt:
