Ben's plant sat by the sunny window and grew tall. He moved it to a dark corner for a week. When he looked again, the plant was droopy and short.

What most likely made the plant droop?
choices: It lost its sunlight in the corner
Ben gave it too much water
It grew too tall too fast
questions.correct: It lost its sunlight in the corner
questions.hint: Notice what changed for the plant between when it was tall and when it drooped. Connect that change to how it looks now.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! The plant grew tall in the sun, then drooped in the dark corner without it. Nice work!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Hmm, the plant was happy by the sunny window. What did it lose in the dark corner? Think about that.
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! The plant drooped because it lost its sunlight in the corner. Now you know!

standards.standard_description: Ask and answer questions to help determine or clarify the meaning of words and phrases in a text
standards.parent_tip: When you find a tricky word, read the sentence around it together and guess what it means.

questions.prompt: The text says, "A fish's habitat is water." What does the word 'habitat' mean?
choices: How fast a fish can swim
The place where a fish lives
What a fish likes to eat
Where a fish goes to play
questions.correct: The place where a fish lives
questions.hint: Think about where a fish is found if its 'habitat is water'.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! A habitat is the place where an animal lives, like water for a fish. Nice work!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Good try! The text says a fish's habitat IS water. Water is a place. So what does habitat mean?
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! Habitat means the place where a fish lives. Now you know!

questions.prompt: Dolphins need to find food even when the water is very dark. They use echolocation. This helps them send out sounds that bounce back, showing them where fish are hiding.
choices: B: making loud noises to scare fish away
C: using sounds to find things
D: sleeping quietly in the water
questions.correct: C: using sounds to find things
questions.hint: Look at how the sounds help the dolphins find fish in the dark water.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! Echolocation is using sounds that bounce back to find things. Nice work!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Almost! The text says dolphins send out sounds that bounce back. What are the sounds helping them do?
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! Echolocation means using sounds to find things. Now you know!

questions.prompt: A squirrel lives in a tree. A fish lives in the water. A bird lives in a nest. What does the word 'habitat' mean when it says, 'Each animal has its own special habitat'?
choices: The place where an animal lives
questions.correct: The place where an animal lives
questions.hint: Look at where the squirrel, fish, and bird make their homes to understand what 'habitat' means.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! A tree, water, and a nest are all places. A habitat is where an animal lives. Nice work!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Close one! A tree, water, and a nest are all what? They are places to live. So what is a habitat?
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! Habitat means the place where an animal lives. Now you know!

questions.prompt: The hermit crab needed a new shell because it had outgrown its old one. If you wanted to understand what 'outgrown' means, which question would help you the most?
choices: What color was the old shell?
Was the crab too big for its old shell?
Did the crab like its old shell?
Where did the crab find its new shell?
questions.correct: Was the crab too big for its old shell?
questions.hint: Look at the part of the sentence that tells why the crab needed a new shell.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! The crab grew too big for its shell, so that question helps you learn 'outgrown.' Nice work!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Hmm, 'outgrown' means it got too big. Which question asks about the crab's SIZE? That one helps most.
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! The best question is whether the crab was too big for its old shell. That shows what 'outgrown' means. Now you know!

questions.prompt: The story says: "The whale was ENORMOUS — its tail alone was as long as a school bus!" What does ENORMOUS mean?
questions.hint: Look at the clue right after the word — a tail as long as a school bus. What does that tell you about the whale's size?
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! A tail as long as a school bus is huge, so enormous means very big. Nice work!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Take another look! The tail was as long as a school bus. Is that small or huge? Picture it.
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! Enormous means very big, like that whale. Now you know!

questions.prompt: The passage says starfish can regenerate. If a starfish loses an arm, what can it do next?
choices: It can swim faster to escape.
It can grow a new arm.
questions.correct: It can grow a new arm.
questions.hint: To understand what 'regenerate' means, look at the sentence in the passage that uses this word.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! Regenerate means to grow back, so the starfish can grow a new arm. Nice work!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Not yet! Regenerate means to grow something back. If it lost an arm, what could it grow again?
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! If a starfish loses an arm, it can grow a new arm. Now you know!

questions.prompt: Read this: "The little seeds were dormant all winter. They did not grow. They did not move. When spring came, they finally woke up and sprouted." What does the word 'dormant' mean?
questions.hint: Look at what the seeds did AFTER winter. Did they stay the same, or did something change?
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! The seeds did not grow or move, so dormant means resting and not growing. Nice work!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Good try! The seeds did not grow and did not move all winter. What word matches that? Think again.
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! Dormant means resting and not growing. Now you know!

questions.prompt: Read this: "Ben's puppy had not eaten all day. When Ben set down the bowl, the puppy wolfed down its food in one big rush." What does 'wolfed down' mean?
questions.hint: Think about how a puppy that missed meals all day would eat. The word 'rush' is a clue.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! The hungry puppy ate in one big rush, so 'wolfed down' means ate very fast. Nice work!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Hmm, the puppy had not eaten all day and ate in one big rush. Was that slow or fast? Think about it.
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! 'Wolfed down' means ate very fast. Now you know!

questions.prompt: Read this: "In summer, a big swarm of bees flew over the field. There were so many bees that the sky looked dark. They all buzzed together in one giant cloud." What does the word 'swarm' mean?
questions.hint: The words 'so many' and 'all together' tell you about how many bees there were.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! So many bees flying together is a swarm, a large group flying as one. Nice work!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Almost! There were SO many bees the sky went dark. Was that one bee or lots together? Reread it.
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! A swarm means a large group flying together. Now you know!

questions.prompt: Read this: "Ants are tiny, but they never work alone. A whole colony shares one home in the dirt. Together they carry a leaf much bigger than any single ant." What does the word 'colony' mean?
choices: A group of ants living together
questions.correct: A group of ants living together
questions.hint: Ask yourself: does 'colony' name one ant, a place, or many ants? Look at 'whole' and 'together.'
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! A whole group of ants sharing one home is a colony. Nice work!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Close one! The text says a WHOLE group shares one home and works together. What word fits that group?
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! A colony means a group of ants living together. Now you know!

questions.prompt: Read this: "Grandpa said his old truck was on its last legs. It broke down almost every week. Soon it would not run at all." What does the phrase 'on its last legs' mean?
choices: Almost worn out and ready to stop
questions.correct: Almost worn out and ready to stop
questions.hint: A truck has no real legs, so use the other clues: it broke down a lot and soon would not run.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! The truck broke down a lot and would soon stop, so it was almost worn out. Nice work!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Not yet! The old truck broke down every week and would soon stop running. Is it new or almost done?
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! 'On its last legs' means almost worn out and ready to stop. Now you know!

standards.standard_description: Know and use various text features to locate key facts or information in a text
standards.parent_tip: Show your child how to use a table of contents, headings, and bold words to find information quickly.

questions.prompt: Listen to the story: Ben wanted to know what page Chapter 3 started on. The table of contents at the front of his book lists each chapter and the page it starts on. The glossary and index at the back only list words and topics, and the cover just shows the title. Where would you look to find what page a chapter starts on?
questions.hint: There's a special list near the front of a book that shows where each chapter starts.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! The table of contents lists each chapter and its page. Nice work!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Hmm, which part at the FRONT lists each chapter and the page it starts on? That is where to look.
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! You find a chapter's page in the table of contents. Now you know!

questions.prompt: Listen to the story: Each part of Mia's book had a heading in big letters at the top. The heading told what that section was about, like 'Baby Animals.' It did not say who wrote the book, how many pages were left, or when it was made. What does a heading at the top of a page tell you?
questions.hint: A heading is like a mini title at the start of a section.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! A heading tells you what that section is about. Nice work!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Good try! A heading in big letters, like 'Baby Animals,' tells you what? Think about what it names.
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! A heading tells what that section is about. Now you know!

questions.prompt: A glossary in the back of a book helps you:
questions.hint: A glossary is like a mini dictionary at the back.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! A glossary lists words and helps you learn what they mean. Nice work!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Take another look! A glossary is like a little dictionary in the back. What does it help you with?
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! A glossary helps you learn word meanings. Now you know!

questions.prompt: If a word in a book is in bold (dark, thick letters), it probably means:
choices: It is an important word to know
questions.correct: It is an important word to know
questions.hint: Authors make words bold to show they are important.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! Bold letters make a word stand out because it is important to know. Nice work!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Almost! Why would a book make a word dark and thick? It wants you to notice it. Why might that be?
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! A bold word probably means it is an important word to know. Now you know!

questions.prompt: You want to learn about **Polar Bears** in a book about animals. Where do you look first?
choices: Check the table of contents or index
questions.correct: Check the table of contents or index
questions.hint: Use text features to find topics quickly without reading everything.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! The table of contents or index helps you find polar bears fast. Nice work!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Not yet! What part helps you FIND a topic quickly instead of reading the whole book? Look there first.
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! Check the table of contents or index to find it fast. Now you know!

questions.prompt: Tim is reading a book about the ocean. He sees the word tide, but he does not know what it means. He wants to find out what the word means. Where in the book should Tim look?
questions.hint: You want to know what a word MEANS, not what page it is on. Which part gives the meaning of words?
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! A glossary lists words and tells what they mean, so Tim can look up 'tide' there. Nice work!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Close one! Tim wants to know what a WORD means. Which part gives word meanings? Think about that.
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! Tim should look in the glossary to find what 'tide' means. Now you know!

questions.prompt: Rosa's book has many chapters. She wants to find EVERY page that talks about rain, even pages hidden inside other chapters. Which part of the book will help her most?
questions.hint: You need every single page a topic shows up on, not just where each chapter begins.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! The index lists every page that talks about a topic, even hidden ones. Nice work!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Hmm, Rosa wants EVERY page about rain, even ones inside other chapters. Which part lists all those pages?
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! The index helps her find every page about rain. Now you know!

questions.prompt: On one page there is a big photo of a spider. Right under the photo is one short line of words. What do those words most likely do?
choices: They tell who owns the book
They tell how long the book is
questions.hint: Think about where the words are. Words sitting right under a picture usually go with that picture.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! Words right under a photo are a caption that tells about the picture. Nice work!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Good try! The words sit right under the spider photo. What would those words most likely be about?
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! Those words tell about the photo. They are a caption. Now you know!

questions.prompt: In her science book, Kim sees the word roots printed in thick, dark letters while the words around it are thin. Why did the book make the word roots look different?
choices: To show who wrote the book
To show it is spelled wrong
To show the book is ending
To show it is an important word
questions.correct: To show it is an important word
questions.hint: Books make a word bold on purpose. Ask yourself what a book wants you to notice about that word.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! Making a word thick and dark shows it is an important word to know. Nice work!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Take another look! Why make one word thick and dark while others are thin? The book wants you to notice it. Why?
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! The word looks different to show it is an important word. Now you know!

questions.prompt: A page has the heading Parts of a Plant. It shows a picture of a plant. Small words point to the stem, the leaf, and the roots. What do those small words do?
choices: They name the parts of the plant
They tell the whole book's name
They tell what page you are on
questions.correct: They name the parts of the plant
questions.hint: The little words point right at pieces of the picture. What would words that point at things be telling you?
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! The small words point to each part and name them, like stem, leaf, and roots. Nice work!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Almost! The little words point to the stem, leaf, and roots. What are those words doing for each part?
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! Those small words name the parts of the plant. Now you know!

standards.standard_description: Distinguish between information provided by pictures or illustrations and information provided by words in a text
standards.parent_tip: Ask: What did you learn from the words? What extra information does the picture give you?

questions.prompt: The text says "Ladybugs are small insects." The picture shows a ladybug on a green leaf. What does the PICTURE tell us that the words do NOT?
choices: The ladybug is on a green leaf
questions.correct: The ladybug is on a green leaf
questions.hint: What extra detail do you see in the picture that the words don't say?
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! The words never say where the ladybug is, but the picture shows it on a green leaf. Nice work!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Hmm, the words already told us ladybugs are small insects. What EXTRA thing can you only see in the picture?
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! The picture shows the ladybug is on a green leaf, which the words did not say. Now you know!

questions.prompt: Listen to the story: In Sam's book, the words told about a bird. A picture cannot make sounds or smells, and feelings are told by words. But the picture showed the exact colors and shapes of the bird, its bright red feathers and round body. What can pictures in a book show that words cannot?
questions.hint: Pictures let you SEE things — what can you see that's hard to describe?
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! Pictures can show exact colors and shapes that words cannot. Nice work!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Good try! A picture cannot make sounds or smells. But you can SEE something in it. What can you see?
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! Pictures can show exact colors and shapes. Now you know!

questions.prompt: The words say "The bird has feathers." The picture shows a bright blue bird. What does the picture add?
questions.hint: The words didn't mention the color — where did you learn that?
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! The words say 'feathers,' but only the picture shows the bird is blue. Nice work!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Not yet! The words already told us about feathers. What NEW thing does the picture show you? Look at its color.
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! The picture adds that the bird is blue. Now you know!

questions.prompt: You are reading a book about flowers. The words say, 'Flowers are pretty. They grow tall and have colorful petals.' The picture next to the words shows a flower with its roots under the ground. What can you learn only from the picture?
questions.hint: Remember to look at what the drawing shows that the sentences do not mention.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! The words never say anything about roots, but the picture shows them under the ground. Nice work looking at both!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Hmm, some of those things are right there in the words. Look for the one thing you can ONLY see in the picture.
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! The answer is 'Flowers have roots.' The words don't say it, but the picture shows roots under the ground. Now you know!

questions.prompt: The words say "The house was old." The picture shows a house with broken windows and peeling paint. The PICTURE tells us:
questions.hint: The picture is filling in DETAILS the words don't show. What does the picture tell you?
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! The words just say old, but the picture shows us HOW it looks old, with broken windows and peeling paint. Nice work!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Almost! The words already told us the house is old. Think about what EXTRA thing the picture shows you.
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! The answer is 'HOW the house looks old.' The picture shows the broken windows and peeling paint. Now you know!

questions.prompt: The words say: "The fox naps in its den." The picture shows a red fox curled up asleep with two baby foxes next to it. Which fact do we learn ONLY from the picture, not from the words?
choices: The fox is red and has two babies
The fox is in its den
questions.correct: The fox is red and has two babies
questions.hint: Cross out every fact the words already tell you. What is left can only come from the picture.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! The words tell us the fox naps in its den, but only the picture shows the color and the babies. Nice work!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Good try! Some of those are things the words already tell us. Look for what you can ONLY see in the picture.
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! The answer is 'The fox is red and has two babies.' Only the picture shows that. Now you know!

questions.prompt: The picture shows a big gray whale jumping out of blue water. The words say: "A whale is not a fish. It breathes air." Which fact can you learn ONLY from the words, not the picture?
questions.hint: Ask yourself: could a picture ever show this? If your eyes can't see it, it must come from the words.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! You can see the whale in the picture, but you cannot see it breathe air. Only the words tell us that. Nice work!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Close one! Some of those are things you can SEE in the picture. Look for the fact only the words can tell you.
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! The answer is 'A whale breathes air.' You cannot see that in a picture, only the words tell it. Now you know!

questions.prompt: The words say: "Bees make honey in a hive." The picture shows one bee on a yellow flower, far away from any hive. Which fact do BOTH the words and the picture tell you?
questions.hint: Find the one thing that shows up in the words AND in the picture. The others come from just one place.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! The words and the picture both tell us about the bee. Nice work matching them up!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Not yet! Look for the ONE thing that is in the words AND in the picture. What do you see and read in both?
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! The answer is 'There is a bee.' The words say bee and the picture shows a bee too. Now you know!

questions.prompt: The words say: "The dog is small." The picture shows a small brown dog wearing a red hat. Tom wants to know what color the dog's hat is. Where should Tom look?
choices: Neither one tells about the hat
The words, because they describe the dog
Both the words and the picture
The picture, because it shows the hat's color
questions.correct: The picture, because it shows the hat's color
questions.hint: Think about which source could answer the hat question. The words talk about the dog, but do they mention a hat?
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! The words only talk about the dog, but the picture shows the hat's color. That's the best place to look. Nice work!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Take another look! Tom wants to know a COLOR. Which one can show him a color, the words or the picture?
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! Tom should look at the picture, because it shows the hat's color. Words tell, but pictures show colors. Now you know!

questions.prompt: The picture shows a boy in a raincoat holding an umbrella. The words say: "It rained all morning. The boy felt cold." Which fact can you learn ONLY from the words, not the picture?
choices: There is an umbrella and a raincoat
questions.hint: A picture can show clothes and things, but can it show how someone feels inside?
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! You can see the raincoat and umbrella in the picture, but only the words tell us how the boy feels. Nice work!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Good try! Some of those things you can SEE in the picture. Look for the feeling that only the words can tell you.
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! The answer is 'The boy felt cold.' You cannot see a feeling, only the words tell it. Now you know!

standards.standard_description: Use illustrations and details in a text to describe its key ideas
standards.parent_tip: Look at pictures together and ask: What is this teaching us? How does it connect to what we just read?

questions.prompt: A book about seasons shows a picture of trees with no leaves and snow on the ground. This picture shows:
questions.hint: Snow and bare trees are clues about which season.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! No leaves on the trees and snow on the ground are clues for the cold season. Nice work reading the picture!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Hmm, think about the snow and the bare trees. Which season is cold and snowy like that?
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! The answer is Winter. Snow and bare trees are winter clues. Now you know!

questions.prompt: A book about healthy food shows a picture of a plate with fruits, vegetables, and grains. The picture helps you understand:
choices: What a balanced meal looks like
questions.correct: What a balanced meal looks like
questions.hint: The picture shows you an example of healthy eating.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! Fruits, vegetables, and grains all on one plate show a healthy, balanced meal. Nice work!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Almost! The book is about healthy food, and the picture shows a full plate. What does that plate help you understand?
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! The picture helps you understand what a balanced meal looks like. All the food groups are on one plate. Now you know!

questions.prompt: Imagine a map in a book about lions. It shows Africa with different colored areas. Next to the map, a small box, called a key, tells what each color stands for. This map helps you understand:
choices: The kinds of food lions eat.
Where in Africa lions can be found.
How big a lion's family is.
questions.correct: Where in Africa lions can be found.
questions.hint: Think about what the colored areas on a map, explained by a key, usually show about animals.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! A map with a key shows places, so it tells where lions live in Africa. Nice work!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Take another look! A map shows PLACES, not sounds or food. What can a map of Africa help you find out?
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! The map helps you understand where in Africa lions can be found. Maps show places. Now you know!

questions.prompt: A book about the water cycle has arrows showing water going from the ocean to clouds to rain. The arrows help you understand:
choices: The order of the water cycle
questions.correct: The order of the water cycle
questions.hint: Arrows show direction and order — what happens first, next, last.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! The arrows point from step to step, so they show the order of the water cycle. Nice work!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Close one! Arrows point one way to the next. Think about what the arrows show you about the steps.
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! The arrows help you understand the order of the water cycle. They point from one step to the next. Now you know!

questions.prompt: Imagine you are reading an informational book about animals. On one page, there is a big picture of a lion with a shaggy mane. The words on that page say, 'Lions are powerful cats that live in Africa.' What does the picture help you understand about lions?
choices: That lions like to be alone.
What a powerful cat with a shaggy mane looks like.
How many cubs a lion has.
questions.correct: What a powerful cat with a shaggy mane looks like.
questions.hint: Think about how pictures in books help you understand what the words are describing.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! The picture shows you what that strong cat with the shaggy mane really looks like. Nice work!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Hmm, the picture can only show what you can SEE. It cannot show sleeping spots or how many cubs. What does it show?
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! The picture helps you understand what a powerful cat with a shaggy mane looks like. Now you know!

standards.standard_description: Identify the reasons an author gives to support points in a text
standards.parent_tip: Ask your child: What is the author trying to tell us? What reasons did they give?

questions.prompt:
You should drink water every day. Water helps your body work well and keeps you from getting thirsty.

What reason does the author give for drinking water?
choices: Water helps your body work well
questions.correct: Water helps your body work well
questions.hint: The author gives a reason WHY — look for it after the first sentence.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! The author tells us water helps your body work well. That's the reason to drink it. Nice work!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Good try! A reason tells WHY you should do it. Read again and find why the author says to drink water.
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! The reason is that water helps your body work well. That's why the author says to drink it. Now you know!

questions.prompt:
Dogs make great pets. They are loyal, playful, and love their owners.

What reasons does the author give?
choices: Dogs are loyal, playful, and loving
questions.correct: Dogs are loyal, playful, and loving
questions.hint: The author lists reasons after saying dogs are great pets.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! The author says dogs are loyal, playful, and love their owners. Those are the reasons. Nice work!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Almost! Look for the words the author actually wrote about dogs. What three nice things did the author say?
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! The author says dogs are loyal, playful, and loving. Those are the reasons dogs make great pets. Now you know!

questions.prompt: Listen to the story: In her book, the author says kids should eat their vegetables. She gives one reason: vegetables have vitamins that help you grow strong. She does not say they taste sweet, and she never talks about sharing food or growing taller. What reason does the author give to show why kids should eat vegetables?
choices: Vegetables have vitamins that help you grow strong.
questions.correct: Vegetables have vitamins that help you grow strong.
questions.hint: Look at the second sentence of the passage to find out why the author says to eat vegetables.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! The author gives one reason: vegetables have vitamins that help you grow strong. Nice work listening!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Take another look! The story said what the author does NOT say. Find the one real reason about vegetables.
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! The reason is that vegetables have vitamins that help you grow strong. That's the one the author gives. Now you know!

questions.prompt:
Recycling is important. It reduces trash and helps keep the Earth clean.

What reason does the author give for recycling?
choices: It reduces trash and keeps Earth clean
questions.correct: It reduces trash and keeps Earth clean
questions.hint: The second sentence gives the reasons.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! The author says recycling reduces trash and keeps Earth clean. That's the reason. Nice work!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Close one! A reason tells WHY recycling is important. Read again and find what recycling does.
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! The reason is that it reduces trash and keeps Earth clean. That's why recycling matters. Now you know!

questions.prompt:
Reading every day makes you smarter. It helps you learn new words and understand more stories.

The author supports the point by saying reading:
choices: Helps you learn words and understand stories
questions.correct: Helps you learn words and understand stories
questions.hint: What GOOD things does the author say happen when you read?
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! The author says reading helps you learn words and understand stories. Nice work finding the reason!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Not yet! Look for the good things the author says reading does for you. What does reading help you do?
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! The author says reading helps you learn words and understand stories. Now you know!

questions.prompt:
Bats are helpful animals. They eat bugs that hurt our gardens. Bats sleep upside down in caves.

Which sentence tells WHY bats are helpful?
choices: They eat bugs that hurt our gardens
Bats sleep upside down in caves
questions.correct: They eat bugs that hurt our gardens
questions.hint: A reason answers "why?" Which sentence would finish the sentence "Bats are helpful because ___"?
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! Eating bugs that hurt our gardens is WHY bats are helpful. Nice work finding the reason!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Good try! One sentence tells WHY bats help us. Sleeping in caves is not a help. Which one is?
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! Bats are helpful because they eat bugs that hurt our gardens. That's the WHY. Now you know!

questions.prompt:
Winter can be fun. You can slide down a snowy hill. Winter is also very cold. You should wear a warm coat so you do not shiver.

What reason does the author give for wearing a warm coat?
choices: So you can slide down the hill
questions.hint: Find the point the coat sentence goes with. Is a coat about the FUN part or the COLD part?
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! You wear a warm coat so you do not shiver in the cold. Nice work finding the reason!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Almost! A coat keeps you warm. Read again and find WHY the author says to wear one.
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! The reason is so you do not shiver. A warm coat keeps you from getting cold. Now you know!

questions.prompt:
Milk is good for kids. It makes your bones strong. It also helps your teeth grow well.

The author gives these reasons to show that ____.
questions.hint: The two reasons both back up one big idea. What is that big idea?
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! Strong bones and good teeth are reasons that show milk is good for kids. Nice work!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Take another look! The two reasons both point to one big idea. What are these reasons trying to prove?
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! The reasons show that milk is good for kids. Strong bones and good teeth prove it. Now you know!

questions.prompt:
You should wash your hands before you eat. Clean hands keep germs away.

Which sentence gives ANOTHER good reason to wash your hands?
choices: Water comes out of the sink
The soap is a nice color
Clean hands help keep you from getting sick
questions.correct: Clean hands help keep you from getting sick
questions.hint: A reason has to tell why washing hands is a good idea, not just say something about hands or soap.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! Staying from getting sick is another great reason to wash your hands. Nice work!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Close one! A good reason to wash tells why it helps YOU. The sink and the fingers don't. Which one helps?
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! Another good reason is that clean hands help keep you from getting sick. Now you know!

questions.prompt:
Owls hunt at night. Their big eyes help them see in the dark. Their soft feathers let them fly so quietly that mice never hear them coming.

Why can an owl sneak up on a mouse without being heard?
choices: Its soft feathers let it fly quietly
Its big eyes help it see in the dark
questions.correct: Its soft feathers let it fly quietly
questions.hint: The author gives two different reasons. Pick the one about being QUIET, not the one about seeing.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! Soft feathers let the owl fly so quietly the mouse never hears it. Nice work finding the reason!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Good try! The question asks why the owl is QUIET. Big eyes help it see, but what helps it stay silent?
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! The owl can sneak up because its soft feathers let it fly quietly. Now you know!

standards.standard_description: Identify basic similarities in and differences between two texts on the same topic
standards.parent_tip: Read two short books on the same topic and ask: What's the same? What's different?

questions.prompt: Book 1 says dogs need walks. Book 2 says dogs need exercise. These books are:
choices: Saying the same thing in different words
questions.correct: Saying the same thing in different words
questions.hint: Walks and exercise — are those similar?
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! A walk IS exercise, so both books mean the same thing with different words. Nice work!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Hmm, a walk gives a dog exercise. Are the books really saying different things, or the same idea?
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! The books are saying the same thing in different words. A walk is exercise. Now you know!

questions.prompt: Book 1 says the sun is a big star. Book 2 says the sun is a star in the sky. What do both books agree about the sun?
questions.hint: Look closely at what both Book 1 and Book 2 call the sun.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! Both books call the sun a star, so that's what they agree about. Nice work!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Almost! Read both books again. What is the ONE word both of them use for the sun?
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! Both books agree the sun is a star. They both use that word. Now you know!

questions.prompt: Book 1 is about pet dogs. Book 2 is about wild wolves. These books are DIFFERENT because:
choices: One is about a pet, one is about a wild animal
questions.correct: One is about a pet, one is about a wild animal
questions.hint: One animal lives with people, the other doesn't.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! One book is about a pet dog and one is about a wild wolf. That's how they are different. Nice work!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Take another look! A pet and a wild animal are not the same. Think about how the two books are different.
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! The books are different because one is about a pet, one is about a wild animal. Now you know!

questions.prompt: Book 1 tells how rain forms in the sky. Book 2 tells why plants need rain to grow. What is one way these two books are similar?
choices: They both have pictures of animals.
They both talk about the sun.
questions.hint: Think about the main topic that both Book 1 and Book 2 describe.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! One book tells how rain forms and one tells why plants need rain, so both are about rain. Nice work!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Close one! Think about what BOTH books talk about. What one thing is in each book?
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! They are similar because they both are about rain. Now you know!

questions.prompt: Read about Max and Lucy.Max is a big dog. He has brown fur.Lucy is a small dog. She has white fur.What is one thing that is the SAME about Max and Lucy?
questions.hint: Look at the first sentence about Max and the first sentence about Lucy to see what they both are.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! Max and Lucy are different sizes and colors, but they are both dogs. That's the same thing. Nice work!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Not yet! One is big and one is small, so those are different. What is the SAME about both of them?
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! The same thing is that they are both dogs. Now you know!

questions.prompt:
Book 1: "Frogs hop. Frogs live by ponds."
Book 2: "Frogs are green. Frogs live by ponds."
What is the ONE thing BOTH books tell you about frogs?
questions.hint: A true fact for both books must show up in BOTH books, not just one. Check each choice against both.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! Both books say frogs live by ponds, so that's the thing they share. Nice work!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Good try! One fact is in BOTH books. Hopping and green are only in one book each. Which fact is in both?
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! Both books tell you frogs live by ponds. Now you know!

questions.prompt:
Book 1: "Bats fly at night."
Book 2: "Bats fly in the morning."
The two books do NOT agree about one thing. What is it?
questions.hint: Both books say bats fly, so that part matches. Look for the word that is different in each book.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! One book says night and one says morning, so they do not agree about WHEN bats fly. Nice work!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Almost! Both books say bats fly, but they say different TIMES. What are they not agreeing on?
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! The books do not agree about WHEN bats fly. One says night, one says morning. Now you know!

questions.prompt:
Book 1: "Bees make honey. Bees have wings."
Book 2: "Bees have wings. Bees can sting."
Which fact is in Book 2 but NOT in Book 1?
questions.hint: Cross out any fact that shows up in Book 1. What is left is only in Book 2.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! Stinging is in Book 2 only. Book 1 never says it. Nice work comparing them!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Take another look! Find the fact that is ONLY in Book 2. Wings are in both books, so it's not that one.
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! The answer is 'Bees can sting.' It's in Book 2 but not Book 1. Now you know!

questions.prompt:
Book 1: "Rain comes down from the clouds."
Book 2: "On a rainy day, wear boots and a coat."
You want to know what to put on before you go outside in the rain. Which book helps you MORE?
questions.hint: Think about what each book is really about. One tells where rain comes from; the other tells what to do about rain.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! One book tells you to wear boots and a coat, so it helps you get ready. Nice work picking it!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Close one! You want to know what to WEAR outside. One book is about how rain forms. Which book tells about clothes?
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! Book 2 helps you more, because it tells you to wear boots and a coat. Now you know!

questions.prompt:
Book 1: "Ants are the best bugs! They work so hard."
Book 2: "Ants are just okay. They bite you."
How are the two books different?
choices: Book 1 likes ants more than Book 2 does.
Both books think ants are the best.
Book 2 likes ants more than Book 1 does.
Both books say ants can fly.
questions.correct: Book 1 likes ants more than Book 2 does.
questions.hint: Listen to the happy or grumpy feeling in each book's words, not just the facts. Which book sounds like it likes ants?
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! Book 1 calls ants the best, but Book 2 only says they're okay. Book 1 likes them more. Nice work!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Not yet! Read how each book feels about ants. One is very happy about them, one is not. Which book likes ants more?
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! Book 1 likes ants more than Book 2 does. Book 1 calls them the best. Now you know!

standards.standard_description: Recognize the distinguishing features of a sentence
standards.parent_tip: Point out capital letters at the start and periods at the end when reading together.

questions.prompt: A sentence always starts with a:
questions.hint: Look at the first letter of this very sentence — what kind of letter is it?
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! Every sentence starts with a big capital letter. Nice work!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Hmm, think about the very FIRST letter of a sentence. Is it a big letter or a small one?
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! A sentence always starts with a capital letter. The first letter is always big. Now you know!

questions.prompt: A sentence always ends with a:
choices: Period, question mark, or exclamation point
questions.correct: Period, question mark, or exclamation point
questions.hint: What mark goes at the very end?
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! Sentences end with a period, question mark, or exclamation point. Nice work!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Almost! Think about the mark at the very END of a sentence. A comma or a space doesn't stop it. What does?
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! A sentence ends with a period, question mark, or exclamation point. Now you know!

questions.prompt: Which is a correct sentence?
questions.hint: Capital letter at the start + period at the end = sentence.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! It starts with a capital and ends with a period. That's a correct sentence. Nice work!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Take another look! A good sentence needs a BIG first letter AND an end mark. Which one has both?
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! The correct one is 'The dog ran fast.' It has a capital and a period. Now you know!

questions.prompt: What is wrong with this sentence: **she likes ice cream**
questions.hint: Look at the first letter of 'she.' Should it be lowercase or capital?
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! The word 'she' begins the sentence, so it needs a big capital S. Nice work spotting it!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Close one! Look at the very first letter. A sentence should always START with a big letter. What is missing?
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! It needs a capital S. The first word of a sentence starts with a big letter. Now you know!

questions.prompt: Which sentence needs a capital letter at the beginning?
questions.hint: Check the first letter of each sentence. A sentence always starts with a capital letter.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! That one starts with a small letter, so it needs a capital at the beginning. Nice work!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Take another look! Three of these already start with a big letter. Find the one that starts with a small letter.
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! The answer is 'do you like to play?' It starts with a small d and needs a capital. Now you know!

questions.prompt: Which one is written correctly as a complete sentence?
questions.hint: Check BOTH things: does it start with a capital letter AND end with a period?
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! It has a big first letter AND a period at the end. That's a complete sentence. Nice work!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Good try! A complete sentence needs a capital at the start AND an end mark. Which one has both?
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! The answer is 'The frog can jump.' It has a capital and a period. Now you know!

questions.prompt: One of these sentences is asking something. Which one should end with a question mark?
questions.hint: Read each one and ask: is it telling me something, or is it asking me something?
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! That one is asking about a hat, so it needs a question mark. Nice work hearing the question!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Almost! A question ASKS something. Read each one and find the one that asks, not tells.
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! The answer is 'Where is my red hat.' It asks something, so it needs a question mark. Now you know!

questions.prompt: Which group of words is a COMPLETE sentence?
questions.hint: A complete sentence needs a naming part (who) AND an action or telling part (what they do).
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! It has a naming part and a doing part, so it's a complete sentence. Nice work!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Take another look! A complete sentence needs a WHO and a WHAT they do. Which one tells a whole idea?
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! The complete sentence is 'The cat naps.' It has a who and what it does. Now you know!

questions.prompt: How many sentences are here?  "We ran fast. It was fun. Let's go again!"
questions.hint: Count the end marks — each period, question mark, or exclamation point closes one sentence.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! Each part ends with its own mark, so there are three sentences. Nice counting!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Close one! Count the end marks, like the periods and the exclamation point. Each one ends a sentence. How many?
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! There are 3 sentences. Count the three end marks. Now you know!

questions.prompt: This sentence is missing a capital letter: "my friend has a dog."  Which word should start with a capital letter?
questions.hint: A capital letter goes on the very FIRST word of the sentence — not on the most important word.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! 'my' is the first word, so it needs the big capital letter. Nice work!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Take another look! The capital goes on the FIRST word of the sentence. Which word comes first?
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! The word 'my' should start with a capital. It's the first word. Now you know!

standards.standard_description: Orally produce single-syllable words by blending sounds, including consonant blends
standards.parent_tip: Practice blending: say each sound slowly (/s/ /t/ /o/ /p/) then blend them together fast: stop!

questions.prompt: Blend these sounds together to make a word: /s/ ... /t/ ... /r/ ... /a/ ... /p/. What word do you make?
questions.hint: Say each sound slowly and don't skip any. Listen for THREE sounds crashing together at the start before the vowel.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! /s/ /t/ /r/ /a/ /p/ blends together to make strap. Nice work sounding it out!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Almost! Say each sound slow, then fast: /s/ /t/ /r/ /a/ /p/. Don't drop any sound. What word is it?
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! The word is strap. All five sounds blend together, /s/ /t/ /r/ /a/ /p/. Now you know!

questions.prompt: Blend these sounds together to make a word: /d/ ... /r/ ... /i/ ... /f/ ... /t/. What word do you make?
questions.hint: There is a blend at the START and a different blend at the END. Say the last two sounds carefully after the vowel.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! /d/ /r/ /i/ /f/ /t/ blends together to make drift. Nice work sounding it out!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Good try! Blend each sound slow, then fast: /d/ /r/ /i/ /f/ /t/. Keep all five sounds. What word is it?
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! The word is drift. All five sounds blend together, /d/ /r/ /i/ /f/ /t/. Now you know!

questions.prompt: Which word BEGINS with a consonant blend (two sounds you can hear separately)? shed, chin, sled, thin
questions.hint: A blend lets you hear each letter's sound. A digraph like sh, ch, or th is two letters that make just ONE sound.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! In this word you can hear the /s/ and /l/ as two separate sounds. That's a blend. Nice work!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Not yet! A blend has two sounds you hear one after the other. Some of these make just ONE new sound. Which has two?
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! The answer is sled. You can hear /s/ and /l/ as two sounds. Now you know!

questions.prompt: Blend these sounds together to make a word: /s/ ... /p/ ... /l/ ... /a/ ... /sh/. What word do you make?
questions.hint: Start with three sounds blended together, then the vowel, then one ending sound made by two letters (sh).
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! When you push those sounds together you hear splash, just like water! Nice work!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Hmm, say each sound slowly and blend them: /s/ /p/ /l/ /a/ /sh/. Listen for all five sounds!
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! The word is splash. Say /s/ /p/ /l/ /a/ /sh/ and it melts together. Now you know!

questions.prompt: Blend these sounds together to make a word: /b/ ... /l/ ... /e/ ... /n/ ... /d/. What word do you make?
questions.hint: Count the sounds: there is a blend at the beginning AND a blend at the end. Every sound must show up in your word.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! /b/ /l/ /e/ /n/ /d/ all push together to make blend. Nice work!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Almost! Say each sound slowly and stick them together: /b/ /l/ /e/ /n/ /d/. Did you catch every sound?
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! The word is blend. Slide the sounds together: /b/ /l/ /e/ /n/ /d/. Now you know!

standards.standard_description: Isolate and pronounce initial, medial vowel, and final sounds in spoken single-syllable words
standards.parent_tip: Play sound games: What's the first sound in 'dog'? The middle? The last?

questions.prompt: What is the FIRST sound in the word **map**?
questions.hint: Say 'map' slowly — what sound comes first?
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! Map starts with /m/, like mmm-map. Nice work!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Good try! Say map slowly. What sound do your lips make at the very start?
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! The first sound in map is /m/, mmm-map. Now you know!

questions.prompt: What is the MIDDLE sound in the word **big**?
questions.hint: Say b-i-g slowly — what sound is in the middle?
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! In big, the middle sound is /i/, like b-/i/-g. Nice work!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Close one! Say big slowly and stretch it out. What sound is right in the middle?
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! The middle sound in big is /i/, b-/i/-g. Now you know!

questions.prompt: What is the LAST sound in the word **cup**?
questions.hint: Say 'cup' — what sound do you hear at the very end?
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! Cup ends with /p/, like cu-/p/. Nice work!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Not yet! Say cup slowly. What sound do you hear at the very end?
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! The last sound in cup is /p/, cu-/p/. Now you know!

questions.prompt: Which word has the SAME middle sound as **cat**?
questions.hint: Cat has /a/ in the middle. Which other word has /a/?
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! Cat has the /a/ sound in the middle, and Bat does too! Nice work!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Take another look! Cat has the /a/ sound in the middle. Say each word slowly and find the one that matches.
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! The answer is Bat. Cat and Bat both have /a/ in the middle. Now you know!

questions.prompt: What is the FIRST sound in the word **flag**?
questions.hint: Say the word very slowly. A beginning blend like 'fl' is really TWO sounds squished together — pull off just the very first one.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! Flag starts with /f/, like fff-flag. Nice work!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Hmm, say flag slowly. What is the very first sound your mouth makes? Just the one sound!
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! The first sound in flag is /f/, fff-flag. Now you know!

questions.prompt: What is the FIRST sound in the word **shell**?
questions.hint: Watch out — two letters can team up to make ONE brand-new sound. Say the start of the word and listen for a single hiss, not /s/ then /h/.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! Shell starts with /sh/, like a quiet shhh. Nice work!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Almost! Say shell slowly. The first sound is one quiet sound, like telling someone to be still.
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! The first sound in shell is /sh/, shhh-ell. Now you know!

questions.prompt: What is the MIDDLE sound in the word **chin**?
questions.hint: The middle sound is always the vowel. Skip past the beginning sound (even if it's a two-letter team) and find the vowel in the center.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! In chin, the middle sound is /i/, like ch-/i/-n. Nice work!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Good try! Say chin slowly and stretch it. What sound is right in the middle?
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! The middle sound in chin is /i/, ch-/i/-n. Now you know!

questions.prompt: What is the LAST sound in the word **hand**?
questions.hint: Stretch the word to the very end. An ending blend like 'nd' has two sounds — you want only the last little one you hear.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! Hand ends with /d/, like han-/d/. Nice work!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Close one! Say hand slowly. What is the very last sound, right at the end?
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! The last sound in hand is /d/, han-/d/. Now you know!

questions.prompt: What is the LAST sound in the word **fish**?
questions.hint: Two letters at the end can make ONE sound. Say the word and hold the very last sound — is it a hiss-shush, or two separate sounds?
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! Fish ends with /sh/, like a quiet shhh. Nice work!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Not yet! Say fish slowly. What quiet sound do you hear at the very end?
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! The last sound in fish is /sh/, fi-shhh. Now you know!

standards.standard_description: Segment spoken single-syllable words into their complete sequence of individual sounds
standards.parent_tip: Practice breaking words apart: 'dog' has three sounds: /d/ /o/ /g/. Try it with other words!

questions.prompt: How many sounds in the word **stop**?
questions.hint: /s/ /t/ /o/ /p/ — each letter makes its own sound here.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! Stop has four sounds: /s/ /t/ /o/ /p/. Nice work!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Hmm, tap out each sound in stop: /s/ /t/ /o/ /p/. Count each tap!
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! Stop has 4 sounds, /s/ /t/ /o/ /p/. Now you know!

questions.prompt: Say the word **ship** slowly. How many separate sounds do you hear?
questions.hint: Stretch it out: /sh/.../i/.../p/. Remember that two letters can team up to make just one sound.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! Ship has three sounds: /sh/ /i/ /p/. Remember, /sh/ is just one sound! Nice work!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Good try! Tap out ship slowly: /sh/ /i/ /p/. Careful, /sh/ counts as just one sound!
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! Ship has 3 sounds, /sh/ /i/ /p/. Now you know!

questions.prompt: Say the word **fox** slowly. How many separate sounds do you hear?
questions.hint: Stretch the ending: /f/.../o/.../k/.../s/. Listen for what the letter x really says.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! Fox has four sounds: /f/ /o/ /k/ /s/. The x makes two sounds! Nice work!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Almost! Say fox slowly. Listen close, the x at the end makes MORE than one sound!
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! Fox has 4 sounds, /f/ /o/ /k/ /s/. The x is /k/ and /s/! Now you know!

questions.prompt: Which one of these words has exactly **five** sounds?
questions.hint: Say each word slowly and tap once for every sound. Watch out for letter teams like 'sh', 'th', 'ch', and 'ck' that make just one sound.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! Splash has five sounds: /s/ /p/ /l/ /a/ /sh/. Nice work!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Take another look! Tap out each word slowly and count the sounds. Which one has all five?
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! The answer is splash, /s/ /p/ /l/ /a/ /sh/, five sounds! Now you know!

questions.prompt: Say the word **thank** slowly. How many separate sounds do you hear?
questions.hint: Break it up: the 'th' is one sound, but listen carefully to the ending 'nk' — it hides two sounds.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! Thank has four sounds: /th/ /a/ /n/ /k/. The /th/ is one sound! Nice work!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Close one! Say thank slowly. Remember, /th/ at the start is just one sound. Now count them!
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! Thank has 4 sounds, /th/ /a/ /n/ /k/. Now you know!

questions.prompt: The word **cat** has 3 sounds. Which word below also has exactly **3** sounds?
questions.hint: Tap once per sound for each word. Remember 'ck' is a letter team that makes only one sound.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! Duck has three sounds: /d/ /u/ /k/, just like cat! Nice work!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Hmm, tap out each word and count. Cat has 3 sounds. Which word matches with just 3?
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! The answer is duck, /d/ /u/ /k/, three sounds like cat! Now you know!

standards.standard_description: Read grade-level text orally with accuracy, appropriate rate, and expression
standards.parent_tip: Take turns reading aloud. Model reading with expression — change your voice for different characters!

questions.prompt: When you see an exclamation point (!), you should read with:
questions.hint: Exclamation points mean strong feelings!
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! An exclamation point means read with excitement or big feeling! Nice work!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Good try! An exclamation point is used for big, strong feelings. How would your voice sound then?
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! You read a ! with excitement or strong feeling. It means big feelings! Now you know!

questions.prompt: When you see a question mark (?), your voice should:
questions.hint: Try asking 'Really?' — hear how your voice goes up?
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! When you ask a question, your voice goes up at the end. Nice work!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Almost! Try asking a question out loud, like, Is it time? Listen, which way does your voice go?
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! With a question mark, your voice goes up at the end. Now you know!

questions.prompt: Which of these describes how a good reader sounds when they read a story out loud?
choices: They read every word in a flat voice, all at the same speed.
They read so fast that it's hard to understand what they are saying.
They read with a voice that changes for different parts and takes short breaks at commas.
They read each word very, very slowly, one by one.
questions.correct: They read with a voice that changes for different parts and takes short breaks at commas.
questions.hint: Remember that good readers make their voice match the story and read at a speed that makes sense.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! A good reader changes their voice and takes little breaks at commas. It sounds just right! Nice work!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Take another look! A good reader is not too fast, too slow, or flat. Which one sounds smooth and just right?
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! A good reader changes their voice for different parts and pauses at commas. Now you know!

questions.prompt:
"Help!" cried the little mouse.

How should you read this?
questions.hint: The mouse is crying for help — that sounds scared!
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! The mouse yells Help! so you read it in a loud, scared voice. Nice work!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Hmm, the little mouse is calling Help! How would you feel if you needed help? Read it that way!
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! You read Help! in a loud, scared voice. The mouse needs help! Now you know!

questions.prompt:
Read these four sentences. In which one should your voice take a short PAUSE in the middle?
1. The dog ran fast.
2. Yes, I can help you.
3. We went to the park.
4. She likes to read books.
questions.hint: Look for the little mark that tells your voice to stop for a beat.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! The comma in Yes, I can help you tells your voice to take a little pause. Nice work!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Good try! A pause happens where there is a comma. Look for the sentence with a comma in the middle!
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! The answer is Yes, I can help you. The comma makes you pause. Now you know!

questions.prompt:
Three of these sentences should be read in a happy, excited voice. Which ONE should be read in a sad voice?
1. Hooray, we won the game!
2. I lost my little dog.
3. Look at the big rainbow!
4. It is my birthday today!
questions.hint: Don't just look at the marks — think about how each sentence would make you feel.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! Losing your little dog is sad, so you read it in a sad voice. Nice work!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Close one! Three sentences are happy and exciting. Which one tells about something that would feel sad?
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! The sad one is, I lost my little dog. Losing a pet feels sad. Now you know!

questions.prompt:
In which sentence is a character TALKING out loud, so you would read it in that character's voice?
1. Sam ate his lunch.
2. "I am so hungry," said Sam.
3. The cat was hungry too.
4. It was time to eat.
questions.hint: Look for the marks that go around the exact words someone says.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! The talking marks show Sam is speaking out loud, so use his voice! Nice work!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Take another look! Find the sentence with talking marks around the words a character says out loud.
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! The answer is, I am so hungry, said Sam. The marks show Sam is talking! Now you know!

questions.prompt: Read this sentence out loud: I packed apples, grapes, and nuts. Where should your voice take short pauses?
choices: Only one pause at the very end
In the middle of each word
A short pause after each comma: apples, grapes, nuts
questions.correct: A short pause after each comma: apples, grapes, nuts
questions.hint: Each comma in a list is a tiny stop sign for your voice.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! Each comma tells your voice to take a little pause. Nice work!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Hmm, think about what commas do. They pop up between the things in a list. What do commas tell your voice?
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! You take a short pause after each comma: apples, grapes, nuts. Now you know!

questions.prompt: You are reading a story out loud so a friend can understand it. What is the BEST way to read?
choices: Smoothly, not too fast and not too slow
So slow that each word takes a long time
As fast as you can without stopping
Skip any word that looks hard
questions.correct: Smoothly, not too fast and not too slow
questions.hint: Think about the speed that helps a listener follow along.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! Reading smoothly, not too fast or slow, helps your friend understand. Nice work!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Almost! Too fast, too slow, or skipping words is hard to follow. Which way sounds just right?
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! The best way is smoothly, not too fast and not too slow. Now you know!

standards.standard_description: Use context to confirm or self-correct word recognition and understanding
standards.parent_tip: If your child reads a word wrong, ask: Does that make sense? Encourage them to try again.

questions.prompt: If you read a sentence and it doesn't make sense, a good reader will:
choices: Reread it and try to fix the mistake
questions.correct: Reread it and try to fix the mistake
questions.hint: Good readers check: 'Does that make sense?' and try again.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! When something does not make sense, a good reader rereads and fixes it. Nice work!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Good try! Giving up, skipping, or racing ahead will not help. What could you do to fix a mistake?
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! A good reader rereads it and tries to fix the mistake. Now you know!

questions.prompt: Read the sentence and pick the word that makes sense in the blank: "The frog will ___ from rock to rock."  Choices: hop, hot, hog, hut
questions.hint: Sound out each word, then ask which one is an ACTION a frog can do in the sentence. Let the meaning of the whole sentence decide.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! A frog can hop from rock to rock. That makes sense! Nice work!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Hmm, read it again: The frog will blank from rock to rock. What can a frog really DO?
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! The word is hop, a frog can hop from rock to rock! Now you know!

questions.prompt: Read the sentence and pick the word that makes sense in the blank: "Mom will ___ the bread in the hot oven."  Choices: bake, cake, lake, rake
questions.hint: Read the whole sentence first. Which magic-e word names something you DO to bread in an oven?
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! You bake bread in a hot oven. That makes sense! Nice work!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Close one! Read it again: what do you do to bread in a hot oven? Pick the word that makes sense.
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! The word is bake, you bake bread in the oven! Now you know!

questions.prompt: Sam read this sentence out loud: "Our class went on a ___ to the farm."  He first said the word was 'trap,' but that did not make sense. Which word is really in the blank?  Choices: trip, trap, drip, trop
questions.hint: When a word doesn't make sense, look again at the letters AND ask what fits the sentence. What do you go ON to a farm?
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! A class goes on a trip to the farm. That makes sense! Nice work!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Take another look! Trap did not make sense. What word means going somewhere fun, like the farm?
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! The word is trip, the class went on a trip to the farm! Now you know!

questions.prompt: Read the sentence and pick the word that makes sense in the blank: "The snake will ___ through the tall grass."  Choices: slide, slime, smile, slice
questions.hint: Blend the tricky sl-/sm- beginnings carefully, then keep only the word that tells how a snake MOVES.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! A snake can slide through the grass. That makes sense! Nice work!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Not yet! Read it again: how does a snake move through tall grass? Pick the word that makes sense.
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! The word is slide, a snake can slide through the grass! Now you know!

questions.prompt: Read the sentence and pick the word that makes sense in the blank: "The baby will ___ loudly when she is hungry."  Choices: cry, dry, try, fry
questions.hint: All four rhyme, so sounding out won't be enough. Ask which one a hungry baby would do loudly.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! A hungry baby will cry loudly. That makes sense! Nice work!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Hmm, read it again: what does a baby do loudly when she is hungry? Pick the word that fits.
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! The word is cry, a hungry baby will cry loudly! Now you know!

questions.prompt: Read the sentence: "The dog likes to ___." Which word makes sense in the blank?
questions.hint: Think about what a dog can do. Which word fits with a dog?
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! A dog likes to bark. That makes sense! Nice work!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Good try! Read it again: The dog likes to blank. What sound or thing does a dog do?
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! The word is bark, a dog likes to bark! Now you know!

questions.prompt: Read this: "I put my hat on my ___." Which word makes the sentence make sense?
questions.hint: Where do you wear a hat? Pick the word that fits.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! You put a hat on your head. That makes sense! Nice work!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Close one! Read it again: where do you wear a hat? Pick the word that makes sense.
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! The word is head, you put a hat on your head! Now you know!

questions.prompt: A boy reads: "The cat can jump on the bde." The word "bde" is not right. What word should it be so the sentence makes sense?
questions.hint: A cat jumps on something you sleep on. Fix the funny word.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! A cat can jump on the bed. That makes sense! Nice work!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Take another look! Bde is not a real word. What soft thing could a cat jump on to sleep?
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! The word is bed, a cat can jump on the bed! Now you know!

questions.prompt: Read the sentence: "We eat lunch at ___." Which word makes sense?
questions.hint: When do we eat lunch? Pick the word about time.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! We eat lunch at noon, in the middle of the day. That makes sense! Nice work!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Hmm, read it again: We eat lunch at blank. What word tells a TIME of day?
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! The word is noon, we eat lunch at noon! Now you know!

questions.prompt: A girl reads: "The sun is very hat today." That does not sound right. What word should it be?
questions.hint: How does the sun feel? Fix the word so the sentence makes sense.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! The sun is very hot. That makes sense! Nice work!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Almost! Hat does not sound right for the sun. What word tells how the warm sun feels?
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! The word is hot, the sun is very hot! Now you know!

standards.standard_description: Determine or clarify the meaning of unknown and multiple-meaning words and phrases based on grade 1 reading and content
standards.parent_tip: When your child finds a tricky word, say: Let's read the sentence again and figure out what it means.

questions.prompt: Read the sentence: 'The boy swung the bat to hit the ball.' What does the word 'bat' mean in this sentence?
choices: A flying animal that sleeps during the day.
A stick used to play a game.
questions.correct: A stick used to play a game.
questions.hint: Think about what the boy is doing with the bat in the sentence. What kind of bat do you use to hit a ball?
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! You swing a bat to hit a ball, so bat means a stick for a game! Nice work!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Good try! Read the sentence again. The boy SWUNG the bat to hit a ball. What kind of bat is that?
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! Here, bat means a stick used to play a game, to hit the ball! Now you know!

questions.prompt: Read the sentence: 'When the ball flew at me fast, I had to duck so it would not hit my head.' What does the word 'duck' mean in this sentence?
choices: A bird that swims in a pond.
questions.hint: The same word can mean different things. Look at what the boy DID when the ball came at him, not what a duck is.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! When a ball flies at you, you duck by bending down fast! Nice work!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Close one! Read it again. The ball flew fast at his head. What would you DO so it did not hit you?
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! Here, duck means to bend down quickly so the ball misses you! Now you know!

questions.prompt: The soup on the stove was too hot to eat. Which word means EVEN more hot than 'hot'?
questions.hint: All temperature words are not equal. Think about which one shows the MOST heat, not just some heat.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! Boiling is even hotter than hot, like bubbling soup! Nice work!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Hmm, warm, cool, and wet are not hotter than hot. Which word means super, super hot?
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! Boiling means even more hot than hot, like bubbly soup! Now you know!

questions.prompt: Read the sentence: 'The knot was so tight that Sam could not untie it.' What does the word 'untie' mean?
choices: To pull on it very hard.
To make the knot come loose.
questions.correct: To make the knot come loose.
questions.hint: The little word part 'un-' at the front usually flips a word to mean the opposite. What is the opposite of tying?
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! Un means to undo, so untie means to make the knot come loose! Nice work!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Take another look! The little word un means to undo something. So what does untie a knot mean?
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! Untie means to make the knot come loose. Un means undo! Now you know!

questions.prompt: Read the sentence: 'A big wave splashed over my feet at the beach.' What does the word 'wave' mean in this sentence?
choices: To move your hand to say hello.
Water that rolls onto the shore.
questions.correct: Water that rolls onto the shore.
questions.hint: 'Wave' has more than one meaning. Use the clue words 'splashed,' 'feet,' and 'beach' to pick the right one.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! At the beach, a wave is water that rolls up onto the shore! Nice work!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Good try! Read it again. It splashed over your feet at the beach. What kind of wave does that?
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! Here, wave means water that rolls onto the shore at the beach! Now you know!

questions.prompt: After running all day, the boy was not just tired—he was worn out and could barely walk. Which word best shows he was MORE than tired?
questions.hint: Find the word that shows the STRONGEST amount of tired. Two of the choices actually mean the opposite of tired.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! Exhausted means SO tired you can barely move, even more than tired! Nice work!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Almost! Rested, sleepy, and awake do not mean more than tired. Which word means really, REALLY tired?
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! Exhausted means even more than tired, worn all the way out! Now you know!

standards.standard_description: Use sentence-level context as a clue to the meaning of a word or phrase
standards.parent_tip: Teach your child to be a word detective — the clues to a word's meaning are in the sentence around it!

questions.prompt: Read the sentence: "The dog was soggy after playing outside in the rain." What does the word soggy mean?
questions.hint: Look for the clue in the sentence that tells you it was raining outside.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! The dog played in the rain, so soggy means wet! Nice work!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Hmm, read it again. The dog played outside in the RAIN. How would that make the dog feel?
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! Soggy means wet, like a dog out in the rain! Now you know!

questions.prompt: Read the sentence: "The little ant looked so tiny next to the big rock." What does the word tiny mean?
questions.hint: Find the word in the sentence that means the opposite of big.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! A little ant next to a big rock is tiny, very small! Nice work!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Close one! Read it again. The ant looked little next to a BIG rock. What does tiny tell us?
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! Tiny means very small, like a little ant! Now you know!

questions.prompt: Read the sentence: "Maya felt gloomy when her best friend moved far away." What does the word gloomy mean?
questions.hint: Think about how you would feel if your best friend moved far away.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! Gloomy means sad, just like Maya felt when her best friend moved away. Nice work!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Hmm, think about Maya. Her best friend moved far away. How would that make her heart feel? Try again.
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! Gloomy means sad. Maya missed her friend who moved away. Now you know!

questions.prompt: Read the sentence: "The soup was so scorching that Ben blew on it to cool it down." What does the word scorching mean?
questions.hint: Ask yourself why Ben would blow on the soup to cool it down.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! Scorching means very hot. That is why Ben blew on the soup to cool it! Nice work!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Almost! Ben had to blow on the soup to cool it down. Why would he need to do that? Take another look.
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! Scorching means very hot. Ben blew on it to cool it off! Now you know!

questions.prompt: Read the sentence: "After running and playing all day, Sam was worn out and fell asleep right away." What does the phrase worn out mean?
questions.hint: Use the clues that Sam ran all day and then fell asleep right away.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! Worn out means very tired. Sam ran and played all day, then fell right asleep! Nice work!
