questions.incorrect_feedback: Good try! Sam ran and played all day, then fell asleep right away. How does that make a body feel? Try again.
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! Worn out means very tired. Sam played so much he fell asleep! Now you know!

questions.prompt: Read the story: "The baby birds were ravenous. They chirped as loud as they could and opened their beaks wide the second their mother landed with a fat worm." What does the word ravenous mean?
questions.hint: Ask what the baby birds WANT when they open their beaks wide for the worm, not just what sound they make.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! Ravenous means very hungry. The baby birds opened their beaks wide for the worm! Nice work!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Close one! The baby birds opened their beaks wide the second mom brought a worm. Why did they want it? Take another look.
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! Ravenous means very hungry. The birds wanted that worm to eat! Now you know!

questions.prompt: Read the sentence: "Grandpa carefully peeled the rough brown bark off the tree branch before he began to carve." In this sentence, what does the word bark mean?
choices: the outer skin of a tree
questions.correct: the outer skin of a tree
questions.hint: This word has more than one real meaning. Use the words right around it (tree, branch, peeled) to decide which meaning fits.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! Here bark means the outer skin of a tree. Grandpa peeled it off the branch! Nice work!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Hmm, read it again. Grandpa peeled the rough brown part off a tree branch. What could that part be? Try again.
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! Here bark means the outer skin of a tree. Grandpa peeled it off! Now you know!

questions.prompt: Read the sentence: "The pond was so still that Emma could see her own face in the water, just like looking in a mirror." What does the word still mean here?
questions.hint: Think about what water has to be doing (or not doing) for it to act like a mirror.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! Here still means not moving. The pond was so calm, Emma saw her face like a mirror! Nice work!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Almost! The pond was calm and flat like a mirror. What was the water doing, or not doing? Take another look.
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! Here still means not moving. The calm pond was flat like a mirror! Now you know!

questions.prompt: Read the story: "The path up the hill was very narrow. Ben and his dad could not walk next to each other, so Ben walked in front and his dad walked behind." What does the word narrow mean?
questions.hint: Ask yourself WHY they couldn't walk next to each other. That clue tells you what narrow means.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! Narrow means not wide. The path was too small for Ben and his dad to walk side by side! Nice work!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Good try! Ben and his dad could not walk next to each other on the path. How small was it? Try again.
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! Narrow means not wide. The path was too skinny for two people! Now you know!

questions.prompt: Read the sentence: "Unlike his loud sister who shouted all day, timid little Owen barely said a word and hid behind his mom when guests came over." What does the word timid mean?
questions.hint: The word 'unlike' is a big clue: it means Owen is the OPPOSITE of his loud sister.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! Timid means shy and quiet. Owen barely spoke and hid behind his mom! Nice work!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Close one! Owen barely said a word and hid behind his mom when guests came. How was he feeling? Take another look.
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! Timid means shy and quiet. Owen hid and hardly talked! Now you know!

standards.standard_description: Use frequently occurring affixes as a clue to the meaning of a word
standards.parent_tip: Teach word parts: 'un' means not (unhappy = not happy), 're' means again (redo = do again).

questions.prompt: What is a teacher?
choices: A person who teaches you things
A game you play at school
questions.correct: A person who teaches you things
questions.hint: Think about the word 'teach' and what the ending '-er' means when it's added to a word.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! A teacher is a person who teaches you things. Nice work!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Hmm, think about who helps you learn new things at school. Is it a person, a place, or a thing? Try again.
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! A teacher is a person who teaches you things. Now you know!

questions.prompt: Mom said, "I will reheat the soup for you." The prefix re- means "again." What is Mom going to do to the soup?
choices: Heat it for the very first time
questions.hint: Break the word into its two parts: the prefix re- and the word heat. What does re- add to heat?
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! Re- means again, so reheat means to heat it again. Nice work!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Almost! The prefix re- is your clue. Is Mom warming the soup for the very first time, or one more time? Take another look.
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! Reheat means to heat it again, because re- means again. Now you know!

questions.prompt: Read the sentence: "The baker made warm bread." The word baker ends in -er. What does the -er ending tell you a baker is?
questions.hint: Think about how -er works in words like teacher and painter. Does it point to a person, a place, or a time?
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! The -er ending means a person who bakes. A baker bakes bread! Nice work!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Good try! The -er ending often names a person who does something. So who is a baker? Try again.
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! A baker is a person who bakes. The -er tells you it is a person! Now you know!

questions.prompt: Ben tied his shoe, but it came undone, so he had to tie it a second time. Which word means to tie it AGAIN?
questions.hint: You need the prefix that means "again." Match the prefix to the idea of doing the same thing one more time.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! Retie means to tie again, because re- means again. Nice work!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Close one! You want a word that means to tie one more time. Which prefix means again? Take another look.
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! The word is retie. Re- means again, so retie means to tie again! Now you know!

questions.prompt: Read the sentence: "Maya knocked over her cup because she was not watching what she did." The suffix -less means "without." Which word best describes Maya?
questions.hint: Look at the ending. -less means "without," but -ful means "full of." Which one matches "not watching what she did"?
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! Careless means without care. Maya was not watching, so she knocked her cup over! Nice work!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Hmm, the suffix -less means without. Maya was NOT watching what she did. Which word fits her? Take another look.
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! The answer is careless, meaning without care. Maya was not paying attention! Now you know!

questions.prompt: The prefix pre- means "before." Some children go to preschool. When do they go to preschool?
choices: Instead of ever going to school
After regular school is over for the day
questions.hint: Focus only on what pre- means, then add it to the word school.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! Pre- means before, so preschool comes before regular school. Nice work!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Almost! The prefix pre- gives you a clue about WHEN. Does preschool come first, or later? Take another look.
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! Kids go to preschool before they start regular school, because pre- means before. Now you know!

standards.standard_description: Identify frequently occurring root words and their inflectional forms
standards.parent_tip: Show how one word becomes many: play → plays, played, playing. The root word 'play' stays the same!

questions.prompt: What is the root word in **jumping**?
questions.hint: Take away the -ing. What's left?
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! The root word in jumping is jump. Take off -ing and you get jump! Nice work!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Good try! Cover up the -ing ending. What little word is left over? Take another look.
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! The root word is jump. Take off -ing and jump is left! Now you know!

questions.prompt: What is the root word in **played**?
questions.hint: Take away the -ed ending.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! The root word in played is play. Take off -ed and you get play! Nice work!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Hmm, cover up the -ed ending. What little word is left over? Try again.
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! The root word is play. Take off -ed and play is left! Now you know!

questions.prompt: If you want to describe an action happening *right now*, which word would you make from the root word 'jump'?
questions.hint: Think about how we change root words to show an action that is happening at this very moment.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! Jumping tells us it is happening right now. The -ing shows action happening now! Nice work!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Almost! Which ending shows something is happening right now, this very second? Take another look.
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! The answer is jumping. The -ing ending shows it is happening right now! Now you know!

questions.prompt: **Walks** has the ending "-s." The root word is:
questions.hint: Remove the -s to find the root.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! The root word is walk. Take off the -s and you get walk! Nice work!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Good try! Cover up the -s ending on that word. What little word is left over? Try again.
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! The root word is walk. Take off -s and walk is left! Now you know!

questions.prompt: What is the root word in **runs,** "running," and **runner**?
questions.hint: What word is hiding inside all three words?
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! The root word is run. Runs, running, and runner all start with run! Nice work!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Hmm, what little word do you hear at the start of all three words? Take another look.
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! The root word is run. All three words are built from run! Now you know!

questions.prompt: What is the root word in **stopped**?
questions.hint: Take off the ending that was added. Sometimes a letter gets doubled before you add -ed, so an extra letter comes off too.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! The root word is stop. Take off -ped and you get stop! Nice work!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Almost! Say the word slowly, sound by sound. What little word do you hear at the very beginning? Try again.
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! The root word is stop. Stopped is built from stop! Now you know!

questions.prompt: The words **jumped**, **hopped**, and **walked** all end in **-ed**. What does the **-ed** ending tell you?
questions.hint: Think about WHEN the action happens. Say a sentence: 'I ___ed.' Did it happen now, or is it over?
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! The -ed ending tells us it already happened, in the past! Nice work!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Close one! Jumped, hopped, and walked all finished. Did they happen before now, or later? Take another look.
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! The -ed ending means it already happened in the past. Now you know!

questions.prompt: Which word is built from the root word **help**?
questions.hint: Hide the ending and look at the letters that are left. Which one spells 'help' underneath?
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! Helping is built from help. Just add -ing to help! Nice work!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Good try! Look for the word that has help hiding inside it. Some of these only sound alike! Take another look.
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! The answer is helping. It has the word help right inside it! Now you know!

questions.prompt: Read this: 'One bird sings. Two birds ___.' Which word fills the blank correctly?
questions.hint: We add -s when ONE does the action. Count how many birds there are before you choose.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! Two birds sing. When there is more than one, we use sing! Nice work!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Hmm, there are TWO birds now, not just one. Which word sounds right for more than one? Try again.
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! The answer is sing. Two birds sing, but one bird sings! Now you know!

questions.prompt: The word **replay** means to play again. Which word also has the root word **play**?
questions.hint: Look for the small word 'play' hiding inside the bigger word. The others only START like it.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! Player has the root word play inside it, plus -er! Nice work!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Almost! Look closely for the word play hiding inside. Some of these just start the same way! Take another look.
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! The answer is player. It has play right inside it! Now you know!

standards.standard_description: Demonstrate understanding of word relationships and nuances in word meanings
standards.parent_tip: Play word games: Can you think of a word that means the opposite? A word that means almost the same?

questions.prompt: What is the opposite of **hot**?
questions.hint: If hot means very warm, the opposite would be very not warm.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! The opposite of hot is cold. They are as different as can be! Nice work!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Good try! Think of ice or snow on a winter day. What is the very opposite of hot? Try again.
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! The opposite of hot is cold, like ice! Now you know!

questions.prompt: Which word means almost the same as **big**?
questions.hint: Two of these words mean the OPPOSITE of big. The other one means almost the same.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! Large means almost the same as big. Both mean not small! Nice work!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Hmm, you want a word that means big too, not the opposite. Which one still means big? Take another look.
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! Large means almost the same as big! Now you know!

questions.prompt: What is the opposite of **fast**?
questions.hint: Fast means quick. The opposite would be...not quick.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! The opposite of fast is slow, like a turtle! Nice work!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Almost! Think of a turtle that takes its time. What is the very opposite of fast? Take another look.
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! The opposite of fast is slow, like a turtle! Now you know!

questions.prompt: A bird can fly. Which word tells what a fish can do?
questions.hint: Think about how a fish moves in the water.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! A fish can swim in the water, just like a bird can fly! Nice work!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Good try! Think about where a fish lives. What does it do all day in the water? Try again.
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! A fish can swim in the water! Now you know!

questions.prompt: Sam was so happy that he jumped up and down and yelled for joy! Which word BEST shows how Sam felt?
questions.hint: All four words are happy words. Pick the STRONGEST one that matches jumping and yelling.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! Thrilled shows Sam was SO happy he jumped and yelled! It is the strongest word! Nice work!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Close one! Sam jumped up and down and yelled for joy. Which word shows the BIGGEST happy of all? Take another look.
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! Thrilled shows the biggest happy. Sam was jumping for joy! Now you know!

questions.prompt: Read the sentence: "Dad will park the car by the store." What does the word **park** mean here?
choices: To stop and leave the car
A place with grass and swings
questions.correct: To stop and leave the car
questions.hint: The same word can mean different things. Ask what park does in THIS sentence about a car.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! Here park means to stop and leave the car. Dad parks it by the store! Nice work!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Hmm, park can mean more than one thing. Dad is doing something with the CAR by the store. What is it? Take another look.
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! Here park means to stop and leave the car by the store! Now you know!

questions.prompt: Which word tells us the dog moved the FASTEST?
questions.hint: All four words mean to move. Put them in order from slowest to fastest and pick the fastest.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! Raced shows the dog moved the fastest of all! Nice work!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Almost! Which word shows moving super quick, even faster than a jog? Take another look.
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! Raced means moving the fastest! Now you know!

questions.prompt: Which word means to talk in a very QUIET voice?
questions.hint: All four are ways to use your voice. Three of them are loud. Find the one that is soft.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! Whisper means to talk in a very quiet voice! Nice work!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Good try! The other words are all loud. Which one is soft and quiet instead? Take another look.
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! Whisper means to talk in a very quiet voice! Now you know!

questions.prompt: Read the sentence: "A tear rolled down her cheek." What does the word **tear** mean here?
choices: A drop that comes from crying
To rip a piece of paper
questions.correct: A drop that comes from crying
questions.hint: This word is spelled the same but can mean two things. Use the words 'rolled down her cheek' to decide.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! Here tear means a drop that comes from crying. It rolled down her cheek! Nice work!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Hmm, it rolled down her cheek like a little drop. What comes out when someone cries? Take another look.
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! Here tear means a drop that comes from crying, rolling down her cheek! Now you know!

standards.standard_description: Sort words into categories to gain a sense of the concepts the categories represent
standards.parent_tip: Practice sorting: 'Let's put all the animals in one group and all the foods in another!'

questions.prompt: Dog, cat, and cow are all animals. Which word belongs in the same group?
questions.hint: Say each word and ask yourself: is this a living thing that can move on its own, like a dog or a cat?
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! A bird is an animal, just like dog, cat, and cow! Nice work!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Good try! Dog, cat, and cow are all animals. Which word on the list is also an animal? Try again.
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! A bird belongs with the animals! Now you know!

questions.prompt: Red, blue, and green all go together in one group. What kind of words are they?
questions.hint: Think about what red, blue, and green describe when you look at a crayon or a shirt.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! Red, blue, and green are all colors! Nice work!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Hmm, think about what red, blue, and green really are. You see them everywhere! Try again.
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! Red, blue, and green are all colors! Now you know!

questions.prompt: Apple, banana, and grape are foods. Carrot, corn, and pea are foods too. But they split into two smaller groups: fruits and vegetables. Which word belongs in the SAME group as apple, banana, and grape?
questions.hint: All four words are foods, so 'food' won't help you choose. Ask a smaller question: which one is a fruit?
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! An orange is a fruit, just like apple, banana, and grape! Nice work!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Close one! Apple, banana, and grape are fruits. Which word on the list is a fruit too, not a vegetable? Take another look.
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! An orange is a fruit, so it goes with apple, banana, and grape! Now you know!

questions.prompt: Robin, sparrow, and bluejay belong together in one group. Which name fits this group the BEST?
questions.hint: Every choice might sound true, but pick the smallest, most exact group that fits all three words and leaves out things that don't belong.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! Robin, sparrow, and bluejay are all birds! Nice work!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Almost! Robins, sparrows, and bluejays all fly and have feathers. What ARE they all? Take another look.
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! Robin, sparrow, and bluejay are all birds! Now you know!

questions.prompt: Car, boat, and airplane go together because they are all things you RIDE to travel. Which word belongs in this same group?
questions.hint: Many words are about travel. Only keep the one that is a thing you climb inside and ride, like a car or a boat.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! A train is something you ride to travel, just like a car, boat, and airplane! Nice work!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Good try! You RIDE a car, boat, and airplane. Which word is something else you ride to travel? Take another look.
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! A train belongs in the group, because you ride it to travel! Now you know!

questions.prompt: Snake, worm, and eel go together because they are animals with NO legs. Which word belongs in this group?
questions.hint: Picture each animal. Count its legs. Only keep the one with zero legs, even if another one looks like a snake.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! A slug has no legs, just like a snake, worm, and eel! Nice work!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Hmm, snake, worm, and eel all have NO legs. Which animal on the list also has no legs? Take another look.
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! A slug has no legs, so it goes in the group! Now you know!

questions.prompt: Ben put the word 'bat' in a group with glove, ball, and helmet. In THIS group, what does 'bat' mean?
choices: a stick you swing to hit a ball
an animal that flies at night
questions.correct: a stick you swing to hit a ball
questions.hint: The word 'bat' has more than one meaning. Look at the OTHER words in the group and pick the meaning that matches them.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! In this group, bat means a stick you swing to hit a ball, like in baseball! Nice work!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Close one! Glove, ball, and helmet are all for baseball. What kind of bat goes with those? Take another look.
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! Here bat means a stick you swing to hit a ball, for baseball! Now you know!

standards.standard_description: Define words by category and by one or more key attributes
standards.parent_tip: Practice describing: 'A duck is a bird that swims.' Help your child name the category AND a detail.

questions.prompt: A dog is an animal that:
questions.hint: Think about what makes a dog special compared to other animals.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! A dog barks and has four legs! Nice work!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Good try! Think about a real dog you have seen. What sound does it make, and how many legs? Try again.
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! A dog barks and has four legs! Now you know!

questions.prompt: An apple is a fruit that:
choices: Can be red, green, or yellow
questions.correct: Can be red, green, or yellow
questions.hint: Think about what apples look like.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! An apple can be red, green, or yellow! Nice work!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Hmm, think about apples you have seen at the store. What colors do they come in? Take another look.
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! An apple can be red, green, or yellow! Now you know!

questions.prompt: A hammer is a tool used to:
questions.hint: What do people use a hammer for?
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! A hammer is used to pound nails! Nice work!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Almost! Picture someone building with wood and a hammer. What does the hammer hit? Take another look.
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! A hammer is used to pound nails! Now you know!

questions.prompt: A penguin is a bird that:
questions.hint: What makes penguins different from most birds?
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! A penguin is a bird that swims but cannot fly! Nice work!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Good try! Penguins live where it is icy and love the water. Can they lift up into the sky? Take another look.
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! A penguin swims but cannot fly! Now you know!

questions.prompt: A fire truck is a vehicle that:
questions.hint: What is a fire truck's special job?
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! A fire truck helps fight fires! Nice work!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Hmm, listen to the name: FIRE truck. What big, important job do you think it does? Take another look.
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! A fire truck helps fight fires! Now you know!

questions.prompt: A pond and a lake are both places filled with water. Which sentence tells the KEY thing that makes a pond different from a lake?
choices: A pond is a small pool of water.
A pond is much bigger than a lake.
A pond has salty ocean water.
A pond is always covered in ice.
questions.correct: A pond is a small pool of water.
questions.hint: Both hold water, so ask: what one attribute is TRUE for a pond but NOT for a lake? Think about size.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! A pond is a small pool of water, and a lake is much bigger. Size is the key clue. Nice work!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Hmm, let's think. A lake is BIG. So how is a pond different? Look for the one about its size.
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! The answer is a pond is a small pool of water. A pond is little, a lake is big. Now you know!

questions.prompt: A chair and a stool are both things you sit on. Which sentence tells the KEY attribute that makes something a CHAIR and not a stool?
choices: A chair is found in a kitchen.
A chair has a back to lean on.
A chair is made out of wood.
A chair has legs to stand on.
questions.correct: A chair has a back to lean on.
questions.hint: A stool also has legs and can be wood or in a kitchen. Find the one trait ONLY a chair has.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! A chair has a back to lean on, but a stool does not. That is the key thing. Nice work!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Almost! A stool has legs and can be wood too. Think about the ONE part a stool is missing.
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! The answer is a chair has a back to lean on. A stool has no back. Now you know!

questions.prompt: A bee, an ant, and a beetle all belong to one group. What is the best name for that group, and why?
choices: Fish, because they live in water.
Snakes, because they have no legs.
Insects, because they have six legs.
questions.correct: Insects, because they have six legs.
questions.hint: Name the whole group (category) AND check the attribute that is true for all three little creatures.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! A bee, ant, and beetle are insects, and insects have six legs. Great thinking!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Good try! Fish, snakes, and birds are not right here. Think about a group of tiny bugs with six legs.
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! The answer is insects, because they have six legs. A bee, ant, and beetle all have six legs. Now you know!

questions.prompt: The word 'bat' can name more than one thing. Which sentence uses 'bat' to name an ANIMAL?
choices: The team needs a brand-new bat.
She swung the bat and hit the ball.
He picked a heavy bat for the game.
The bat flew out of the dark cave.
questions.correct: The bat flew out of the dark cave.
questions.hint: Same word, two meanings. Which sentence describes a living thing that belongs in the animal group?
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! The bat flew out of the cave, so that bat is an animal with wings. Nice work!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Close one! Some of these bats are for hitting a ball. Which bat can FLY? An animal, not a toy.
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! The answer is the bat flew out of the dark cave. That bat is a flying animal. Now you know!

questions.prompt: A robin and a penguin are both birds, but they are not the same. Which sentence gives the KEY attribute that makes a robin different from a penguin?
choices: A robin can fly up into the trees.
A robin has two wings and a beak.
A robin has feathers on its body.
A robin lays eggs in a nest.
questions.correct: A robin can fly up into the trees.
questions.hint: Both are birds, so they share many traits. Find the one thing a robin does that a penguin cannot.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! A robin can fly up in the trees, but a penguin cannot fly. That is the key difference. Nice work!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Take another look. A penguin also has wings, feathers, and lays eggs. What can a robin DO that a penguin cannot?
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! The answer is a robin can fly up into the trees. Penguins can't fly. Now you know!

standards.standard_description: Identify real-life connections between words and their use
standards.parent_tip: Connect words to daily life: 'Where is someplace that's cozy? Your bed! What foods are crunchy?'

questions.prompt: Which of these is something COZY?
questions.hint: Cozy means warm and comfortable. Which choice feels warm and comfy?
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! A warm blanket is cozy. Cozy means soft and warm and snug. Nice work!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Not yet! Cozy means warm and snug. A cactus, a rock, and ice are not soft and warm. Which one is?
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! The answer is a warm blanket. A blanket is cozy, soft, and warm. Now you know!

questions.prompt: Which of these is CRUNCHY?
questions.hint: Crunchy means it makes a sound when you bite it.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! Carrots are crunchy. They make a big crunch when you bite them. Nice work!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Almost! Crunchy means it goes CRUNCH when you bite it. Pudding, soup, and yogurt are soft and mushy. Which one crunches?
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! The answer is carrots. Carrots go crunch when you bite them. Now you know!

questions.prompt: Where might you feel CHILLY?
questions.hint: Chilly means a little cold. Where would you feel cold?
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! Outside in the snow you would feel chilly. Chilly means a little cold. Nice work!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Hmm, think again. Chilly means cold. A hot bath, a fire, and a summer beach are all warm. Which place is cold?
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! The answer is outside in the snow. Snow is cold, so you feel chilly. Now you know!

questions.prompt: Which animal is ENORMOUS?
questions.hint: Enormous means very, very big.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! An elephant is enormous. Enormous means very, very big. Nice work!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Good try! Enormous means super big. An ant, a ladybug, and a mouse are all tiny. Which animal is huge?
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! The answer is an elephant. An elephant is enormous, or very, very big. Now you know!

questions.prompt: When might you feel PROUD?
choices: When you do well on a test
questions.correct: When you do well on a test
questions.hint: Proud means feeling good about something you did.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! You feel proud when you do well on a test. Proud is that happy, glad feeling about yourself. Nice work!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Close one! Proud is a great, happy feeling. Being sleepy or breaking a toy is not happy. When do you feel great about yourself?
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! The answer is when you do well on a test. Doing well makes you feel proud. Now you know!

questions.prompt: All of these words mean tired. Which one means the MOST tired — so tired you can barely move?
questions.hint: The words go from a little tired to very, very tired. Picture the most worn-out feeling of all.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! Exhausted is the MOST tired, so tired you can barely move. Nice work!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Almost! Drowsy, sleepy, and tired all mean just a little tired. Which word means the MOST tired of all?
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! The answer is exhausted. Exhausted means super, super tired. Now you know!

questions.prompt: Grandma WHISPERED next to the baby's crib. She whispered because —
choices: She was singing a loud, happy song
The baby was sleeping and she did not want to wake it
She wanted the whole house to hear her
She was very far away outside
questions.correct: The baby was sleeping and she did not want to wake it
questions.hint: Think about a time YOU had to whisper. What was happening around you that made a quiet voice the right choice?
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! Grandma whispered because the baby was sleeping and she didn't want to wake it. Whispering is quiet. Nice work!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Take another look. Whispering is very quiet and soft. Why would you be quiet near a baby's crib?
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! The answer is the baby was sleeping and she did not want to wake it. That's why she was quiet. Now you know!

questions.prompt: Read the sentence: 'In spring, the flowers grow.' Which meaning of SPRING fits this sentence?
choices: Water that comes up from the ground
The season that comes after winter
A bouncy metal coil in a bed
questions.correct: The season that comes after winter
questions.hint: The word 'spring' has four real meanings. Use the OTHER words in the sentence — flowers growing — to decide which one it is here.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! Here spring means the season after winter, when flowers grow. Nice work!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Hmm, spring can mean a few things. But here flowers GROW. Think about the time of year that comes after winter.
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! The answer is the season that comes after winter. In that spring, flowers grow. Now you know!

questions.prompt: The hungry lion ate its whole dinner in a big, fast, hungry way. Which word shows that BEST?
questions.hint: All four are ways to eat, but only one sounds fast and hungry. Which would you do to a tiny snack, and which to a huge meal you couldn't wait for?
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! Gobbled means the lion ate fast in big, hungry bites. Nice work!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Good try! Chewed, nibbled, and tasted are all slow and small. Which word means eating fast in big bites?
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! The answer is gobbled. Gobbled means ate fast in big, hungry bites. Now you know!

questions.prompt: GIGANTIC means very, very big. Which sentence uses the word GIGANTIC the RIGHT way?
choices: The gigantic elephant filled the whole room.
The gigantic ant was too small to see.
The gigantic mouse fit inside my pocket.
questions.correct: The gigantic elephant filled the whole room.
questions.hint: Say each sentence in your head. Three of them put 'very big' next to something tiny — that does not make sense. Find the one that matches.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! The gigantic elephant filled the whole room. Gigantic means very big, and elephants ARE big. Nice work!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Close one! Gigantic means very, very big. A crumb, an ant, and a pocket-sized mouse are tiny. Which sentence uses it for something big?
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! The answer is the gigantic elephant filled the whole room. Gigantic means huge, like an elephant. Now you know!

standards.standard_description: Distinguish shades of meaning among verbs and adjectives differing in manner or intensity
standards.parent_tip: Explore word intensity: Is 'gigantic' bigger than 'big'? Is 'whisper' quieter than 'talk'?

questions.prompt: Which word means MORE than **big**?
questions.hint: One of these means 'extra big.' The others are smaller than big.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! Gigantic means MORE than big. It means super big! Nice work!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Not yet! We want a word bigger than big. Small, tiny, and medium are not big. Which word means the biggest?
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! The answer is gigantic. Gigantic means even bigger than big. Now you know!

questions.prompt: Which word means to move FASTER than **walk**?
questions.hint: Walking is medium speed. What's faster?
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! Run means to move faster than walk. When you run, you go fast! Nice work!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Hmm, think about moving fast. Sit, stand, and crawl are slow or not moving. Which word means to go fast?
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! The answer is run. Running is faster than walking. Now you know!

questions.prompt: Which word shows a STRONGER feeling than **happy**?
questions.hint: Three of these don't really mean happy at all. One means SUPER happy.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! Thrilled is a stronger feeling than happy. It means super happy and excited! Nice work!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Almost! We want a feeling even bigger than happy. Sad, okay, and fine are not that. Which word is the most excited?
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! The answer is thrilled. Thrilled means way more than happy. Now you know!

questions.prompt: Read this: "The soup was hot, the stove was hotter, and the campfire was the hottest of all." Which word means the SAME as very, very hot?
questions.hint: Line the words up from a little heat to the most heat, then pick the very top of the ladder.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! Scorching means very, very hot, hotter than hot. Nice work!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Good try! Cool and warm are not super hot. We want a word for very, very hot. Which one burns the most?
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! The answer is scorching. Scorching means very, very hot. Now you know!

questions.prompt: Ben did not cry just a little. Tears poured down his face and he could not stop for a long time. Which word BEST tells how Ben cried?
questions.hint: Match the word to how HARD he cried in the sentence, and toss out any word that isn't even crying.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! Sobbed means Ben cried hard for a long time. That fits big tears! Nice work!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Take another look. Ben cried a LOT, not a little. Grinned, sniffled, and whimpered are small. Which word means crying hard?
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! The answer is sobbed. Sobbed means crying hard with lots of tears. Now you know!

questions.prompt: The baby was fast asleep, and Mom did not want to wake her. How did Mom most likely talk to Dad?
questions.hint: Use the clue that the baby is sleeping to decide how LOUD Mom would be.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! Mom whispered so she wouldn't wake the sleeping baby. Whispering is quiet. Nice work!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Close one! Mom wanted to stay quiet for the baby. Yelled, screamed, and shouted are all loud. Which word is soft and quiet?
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! The answer is whispered. Whispering is quiet, so the baby stays asleep. Now you know!

questions.prompt: Sara was not just happy about her new puppy — it was the happiest she had ever felt in her whole life! Which word means the MOST happy?
questions.hint: Sort the happy words by how big the feeling is, then grab the very biggest one.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! Thrilled means the MOST happy. Sara felt thrilled about her puppy! Nice work!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Almost! Okay, glad, and pleased mean a little happy. Sara felt the happiest ever. Which word is the biggest happy?
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! The answer is thrilled. Thrilled means the most happy of all. Now you know!

questions.prompt: The dog was so hungry that it ate its whole bowl of food in huge, fast bites. Which word BEST tells how the dog ate?
questions.hint: Match the word to how FAST and how BIG the bites were in the sentence.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! Gobbled means the dog ate fast in big bites. That fits a hungry dog! Nice work!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Not yet! Tasted, nibbled, and licked are slow and tiny. The dog ate fast in huge bites. Which word shows that?
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! The answer is gobbled. Gobbled means ate fast in big bites. Now you know!

standards.standard_description: Use words and phrases acquired through conversations, reading, and responding to texts
standards.parent_tip: Encourage your child to use new words they learn in books: 'Can you use enormous in a sentence?'

questions.prompt: **Because** is a word that tells you:
questions.hint: Try this sentence: 'I wore a coat ___ it was cold.' What does it explain?
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! Because tells you the reason why something happens. Nice work!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Hmm, think about it. Because does not tell who, when, or where. It answers a WHY question. What does it tell?
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! The answer is the reason why. Because tells you why something happened. Now you know!

questions.prompt: Which sentence uses **beautiful** correctly?
questions.hint: Beautiful describes something — it needs a noun after it.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! She saw a beautiful sunset. Beautiful describes the sunset, so it fits. Nice work!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Take another look. Beautiful describes a thing, like a beautiful flower. It can't do actions or be eaten. Which sentence uses it to describe something?
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! The answer is she saw a beautiful sunset. Beautiful describes the sunset. Now you know!

questions.prompt: The soup was too **hot** to eat. Which word means EVEN hotter than hot?
questions.hint: Put the words in order from coolest to hottest, then find the one that goes past 'hot.'
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! Boiling means even hotter than hot, so hot it bubbles! Nice work!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Good try! Mild, warm, and cool are not hotter than hot. Which word means the hottest, like bubbling soup?
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! The answer is boiling. Boiling is even hotter than hot. Now you know!

questions.prompt: Read the sentence: The dog began to **bark** loudly at the mailman. What does **bark** mean here?
choices: A loud sound a dog makes
The rough outside part of a tree
questions.correct: A loud sound a dog makes
questions.hint: The same word can mean different things. Use the rest of the sentence to decide which meaning fits.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! Here bark means the loud sound a dog makes. The dog barked at the mailman! Nice work!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Hmm, bark has a few meanings. But this is about a DOG being loud. What sound does a dog make?
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! The answer is a loud sound a dog makes. The dog barked loudly. Now you know!

questions.prompt: In which sentence does the word **light** mean 'not heavy'?
choices: Her dress was a soft light pink.
He saw a bright light in the sky.
The tiny box was so light that the baby could carry it.
Please turn on the light in your room.
questions.correct: The tiny box was so light that the baby could carry it.
questions.hint: 'Light' has several meanings. Look for the sentence about weight, not about color or brightness.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! The tiny box was so light the baby could carry it. Here light means not heavy. Nice work!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Almost! Light can mean a color or a lamp too. But we want light meaning NOT heavy. Which sentence is about weight?
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! The answer is the tiny box was so light that the baby could carry it. Light there means not heavy. Now you know!

questions.prompt: Lucy screamed and ran when she saw the huge growling bear. Which word best shows how Lucy felt?
questions.hint: All four are real feelings. Pick the strongest one that matches screaming and running away.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! Lucy was terrified. Terrified means very, very scared, so she screamed and ran. Nice work!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Close one! Lucy screamed and ran from a bear, so she was VERY scared. Surprised and annoyed are not that big. Which word is the most scared?
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! The answer is terrified. Terrified means super scared. Now you know!

questions.prompt: Marcus did not want to wake the sleeping baby, so he ___ the words very softly.
questions.hint: Use the clues 'did not want to wake' and 'very softly' to pick the word that means to speak quietly.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! Marcus whispered the words softly so the baby stayed asleep. Nice work!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Take another look. Marcus talked SOFTLY for the sleeping baby. Yelled, shouted, and announced are all loud. Which word is quiet?
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! The answer is whispered. Whispering is soft and quiet. Now you know!

standards.standard_description: Know the spelling-sound correspondences for common consonant digraphs
standards.parent_tip: Two letters can spell one sound! Point them out while reading ('look, sh says shhh!').

questions.prompt: Which word begins with the same sound you hear at the start of 'shoe'?
questions.hint: Think about the first sound you make when you say 'shoe,' and then listen for that same sound at the beginning of each choice word.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! Shell starts with the /sh/ sound, just like shoe. Nice work!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Hmm, say shoe slowly. It starts with /sh/. Which word starts with that same soft /sh/ sound?
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! The answer is shell. Shell and shoe both start with /sh/. Now you know!

questions.prompt: Which two letters make the "chh" sound like in **chip**?
questions.hint: Chip starts with the "chh" sound!
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! The letters ch make the /ch/ sound in chip. Nice work!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Almost! Say the word chip out loud and listen to its very first sound. Which two letters at the start make that sound?
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! The answer is ch. The letters ch make the /ch/ sound in chip. Now you know!

questions.prompt: Which word starts with the **th** sound?
questions.hint: Put your tongue behind your teeth and say thuh!
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! Thumb starts with the /th/ sound. Nice work!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Good try! Say each word slowly. Which one starts by sticking your tongue out for /th/? Not chimp, shell, or what.
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! The answer is thumb. Thumb starts with the /th/ sound. Now you know!

questions.prompt: Look at the word **ship**. What two letters spell one sound?
questions.hint: The first two letters make the "shhh" sound together.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! In ship, the two letters s and h spell one sound, /sh/. Nice work!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Take another look. Two letters at the start of ship team up to spell just ONE sound. Which two letters are they?
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! The answer is sh. The letters s-h spell one sound in ship. Now you know!

questions.prompt: Which word has the **ch** sound at the end?
questions.hint: Listen to the very last sound in each word.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! Beach ends with the /ch/ sound. Say it: bea-ch! Nice work!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Hmm, listen to the END of each word. Fish, bath, and moth end with /sh/ or /th/. Which one ends with /ch/?
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! The answer is beach. Beach ends with the /ch/ sound. Now you know!

questions.prompt: One of these words has the /f/ sound even though there is NO letter 'f' in it. Which word is it?  graph  •  grab  •  grip  •  glad
questions.hint: Two letters can team up to make the /f/ sound. Say each word slowly and listen for the /f/ at the end.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! Graph has the /f/ sound made by the letters p and h, even with no letter f. Nice work!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Good try! Say each word and listen for /f/. Grab, grip, and glad don't have it. Which word sounds like it has an f but doesn't?
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! The answer is graph. In graph, p-h makes the /f/ sound. Now you know!

questions.prompt: Which word ends with the SAME /ch/ sound you hear at the start of 'chip'?  catch  •  cash  •  cast  •  cap
questions.hint: The /ch/ sound at the END of a short word is often spelled with three letters, not two. Say each word and listen for /ch/.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! Catch ends with the /ch/ sound, just like the start of chip. Nice work!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Almost! Listen to the END. Cash ends in /sh/, cast and cap don't have /ch/. Which word ends with /ch/?
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! The answer is catch. Catch ends with the /ch/ sound. Now you know!

questions.prompt: The word 'this' starts with a soft, buzzing /th/ (your voice turns on). Which word starts with that SAME buzzing /th/ — not the quiet /th/ in 'thin'?  them  •  thin  •  thick  •  thumb
questions.hint: Put your hand on your throat and say each word. The buzzing one makes your throat hum on the /th/.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! Them starts with the buzzing /th/ where your voice turns on, just like this. Nice work!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Take another look. Thin, thick, and thumb have the quiet /th/. Which word has the BUZZING /th/ where your voice hums?
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! The answer is them. Them starts with the buzzing /th/, like this. Now you know!

questions.prompt: Three of these words begin with a DIGRAPH (two letters that make ONE sound). Which word does NOT?  shed  •  chin  •  whip  •  spin
questions.hint: A digraph makes one sound; a blend lets you still hear each letter. Stretch out the first two letters of each word.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! Spin does NOT have a digraph. The s and p each make their own sound. Nice work!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Hmm, a digraph is two letters making ONE sound, like sh, ch, wh. Which word has each letter make its OWN sound instead?
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! The answer is spin. In spin, s and p each make their own sound, so it's not a digraph. Now you know!

questions.prompt: Which word has the /sh/ sound (like in 'ship')?  brush  •  catch  •  teach  •  much
questions.hint: Listen closely to the end of each word. /sh/ is a soft, hissing sound; /ch/ has a little punch to it.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! Brush has the /sh/ sound at the end, like ship. Nice work!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Good try! Teach, catch, and much end with /ch/. Which word has the /sh/ sound like ship?
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! The answer is brush. Brush ends with the /sh/ sound. Now you know!

standards.standard_description: Decode regularly spelled one-syllable words
standards.parent_tip: Three-letter words are the foundation. Stretch each sound, then snap them together.

questions.prompt: Which word names a small, furry pet that often says 'meow'?
questions.hint: Sound out each word and think about what animal it names to find the best match.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! A cat is a small, furry pet that says meow. Nice work!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Hmm, think about which pet says meow. A fox, dog, and pig don't meow. Which furry pet does?
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! The answer is cat. A cat is furry and says meow. Now you know!

questions.prompt: Which word rhymes with 'branch'?
questions.hint: Say each word slowly and listen only to the ending part after the first sound. Which ending sounds exactly like '-anch'?
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! Ranch rhymes with branch. They both end in -anch. Nice work!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Almost! Say branch, then say each word. Which one ends with the same -anch sound? Brush, bench, and bunch don't.
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! The answer is ranch. Ranch and branch both end in -anch, so they rhyme. Now you know!

questions.prompt: Three of these words begin with the SAME two-letter blend. Which word does NOT belong?
questions.hint: Stretch out the first two sounds of each word. Three start with /fl/ — find the one that starts a different way.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! Frog does not belong. It starts with fr, but flat, flag, and flip all start with fl. Nice work!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Take another look. Say the first two letters of each. Three start with fl. Which word starts with a DIFFERENT blend?
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! The answer is frog. Frog starts with fr, but the others start with fl. Now you know!

questions.prompt: Which word has the /ch/ sound you hear at the start of 'chin'?
questions.hint: Each word starts with two letters that make one sound. Only one makes the /ch/ sound like 'chin'.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! Chop has the /ch/ sound, just like chin. Nice work!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Good try! Say chin. It starts with /ch/. Thin, when, and shed do not. Which word has that /ch/ sound?
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! The answer is chop. Chop starts with /ch/, like chin. Now you know!

questions.prompt: Which word has the SAME middle vowel sound as 'plum'?
questions.hint: Say each word and hold the middle sound. Which one has the short /u/ sound like the 'u' in 'plum'?
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! Drum has the same middle sound as plum, the /u/ sound. Nice work!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Hmm, say plum and listen to the middle: /u/. Step, clap, and flip have different middle sounds. Which word has /u/?
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! The answer is drum. Drum and plum both have the /u/ sound in the middle. Now you know!

questions.prompt: Which word has only ONE syllable?
questions.hint: Clap once for each vowel sound you HEAR, not for how long the word looks. A long word can still be one clap.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! 'Splash' has just one beat, one vowel sound, so it is one syllable. Nice work!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Hmm, try clapping each word. How many beats do you hear? Pick the one with just one clap.
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! The one-syllable word is 'splash.' It has just one beat. Now you know!

standards.standard_description: Know final -e and common vowel team conventions for representing long vowel sounds
standards.parent_tip: Magic E changes the vowel to say its name. Practice cap/cape, hop/hope, cub/cube.

questions.prompt: Add an E to the end of **cap**. What word do you get?
questions.hint: The E makes the A say its name: AY!
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! Add an E to 'cap' and the A says its name. You get 'cape.' Nice work!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Almost! The E is silent, but it makes the A say its own name. Sound it out again.
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! Cap plus a Magic E makes 'cape.' The E makes the A say its name. Now you know!

questions.prompt: What word do you make when you add a Magic E to the end of **hop**?
questions.hint: Remember what the Magic E does to the vowel sound in the middle of a word.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! Add a Magic E to 'hop' and the O says its name. You get 'hope.' Nice work!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Close one! The Magic E makes the O say its own name. Say the new word slowly.
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! Hop plus a Magic E makes 'hope.' The E makes the O say its name. Now you know!

questions.prompt: Which word has a Magic E?
questions.hint: Magic E is at the end. It makes the vowel say its name!
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! 'Cake' ends in a silent E, and the A says its name. That is the Magic E. Nice work!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Good try! Look for the word that ends with a silent E at the end. Which one has it?
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! The word with a Magic E is 'cake.' It ends in a silent E. Now you know!

questions.prompt: The word **kite** has which vowel sound?
questions.hint: Magic E makes the vowel say its NAME. What does I's name sound like?
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! 'Kite' has a Magic E, so the I says its name and you hear long I. Nice work!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Take another look! The E is silent and makes the vowel say its own name. What does the I say?
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! 'Kite' has the long I sound, like 'eye.' The Magic E makes the I say its name. Now you know!

questions.prompt: What happens to the E in a Magic E word?
questions.hint: Magic E does not make a sound. But it changes the vowel before it!
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! In a Magic E word, the E is silent. You do not say it, but it helps the vowel. Nice work!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Not yet! Say a word like 'cake.' Do you hear the E at the end? Think about that.
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! The Magic E is silent. You never say it, but it helps the vowel say its name. Now you know!

questions.prompt: Read these four words: **gave, wave, have, cave**. Three of them use the Magic E to make the long A sound. Which word is the tricky one that has a SHORT vowel sound instead?
questions.hint: Say each word out loud. Listen for the one where the A does NOT say its name.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! 'Have' looks like a Magic E word, but it tricks us. The A stays short. Nice work!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Good try! Say each word out loud. Three make a long A. Which one sounds short instead?
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! 'Have' is the sneaky one. It has a short vowel, not a long A. Now you know!

questions.prompt: Which word has the long A sound spelled with a vowel team? **rain, ran, ramp, rat**
questions.hint: Look for two vowels sitting together that team up to say the long A.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! 'Rain' uses the vowel team A-I to make the long A sound. Nice work!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Almost! Look for two vowels sitting together that team up to say long A. Which word has that?
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! 'Rain' has the vowel team A-I that makes long A. Now you know!

questions.prompt: Add a Magic E to the end of **plan**. What word do you make?
questions.hint: Keep all the letters of 'plan' and just add e at the end. Don't pick by sound alone.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! Add a Magic E to 'plan' and the A says its name. You get 'plane.' Nice work!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Close one! Just add a silent E to the end so the A says its name. Say the new word.
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! Plan plus a Magic E makes 'plane,' like an airplane. Now you know!

questions.prompt: The word **train** has the long A sound spelled with 'ai'. Which word has that SAME long A sound, but spelled a different way? **play, plan, pat, tap**
questions.hint: Find the word that also says long A, then check which letters make that sound.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! 'Play' has the long A sound spelled with A-Y at the end. Nice work!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Take another look! Which word makes the long A sound, but with different letters than 'ai'?
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! 'Play' has the long A sound, spelled with A-Y. Now you know!

questions.prompt: The word **boat** has the long O sound made by a vowel team. Which word has the SAME long O sound, but made by a Magic E instead? **rope, rock, rob, rot**
questions.hint: Say each word. Find the long O, then look for the silent e at the end.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! 'Rope' makes the long O sound with a Magic E. Nice work!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Good try! Which word ends with a silent E that makes the O say its own name?
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! 'Rope' makes long O with a Magic E at the end. Now you know!

standards.standard_description: Use knowledge that every syllable must have a vowel sound to determine the number of syllables in a printed word
standards.parent_tip: Clap along with words! Each clap is one syllable.

questions.prompt: How many syllables are in the word **cat**?
questions.hint: Clap it out: cat! Just one clap.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! 'Cat' has one beat when you clap it, so it is one syllable. Nice work!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Hmm, clap the word 'cat' out loud. How many claps do you hear?
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! 'Cat' has just one syllable, one clap. Now you know!

questions.prompt: How many syllables are in the word **rabbit**?
questions.hint: Remember that every syllable must have a vowel sound.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! 'Rab-bit' has two beats, so it has two syllables. Nice work!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Almost! Clap the word: rab... bit. Count how many claps you make.
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! 'Rabbit' has two syllables: rab-bit. Now you know!

questions.prompt: How many syllables are in the word **butterfly**?
questions.hint: Remember that each syllable has one vowel sound. Say the word slowly and listen for the vowel sounds.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! 'But-ter-fly' has three beats, so it has three syllables. Nice work!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Close one! Clap it slowly: but... ter... fly. Count each clap.
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! 'Butterfly' has three syllables: but-ter-fly. Now you know!

questions.prompt: How many syllables are in the word 'banana'?
questions.hint: Listen for the separate vowel sounds in the word to count its syllables.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! 'Ba-na-na' has three beats, so it has three syllables. Nice work!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Good try! Clap the word: ba... na... na. How many claps did you make?
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! 'Banana' has three syllables: ba-na-na. Now you know!

questions.prompt: How many syllables in **apple**?
questions.hint: Ap-ple. Two parts, two claps!
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! 'Ap-ple' has two beats, so it has two syllables. Nice work!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Not yet! Clap it out: ap... ple. Count how many beats you hear.
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! 'Apple' has two syllables: ap-ple. Now you know!

questions.prompt: How many syllables are in the word **shine**?
questions.hint: Say the word slowly and count the vowel SOUNDS you hear, not the vowel letters you see. The 'e' at the end is silent.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! 'Shine' has just one beat when you clap it. One syllable! Nice work!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Take another look! The E is silent, so clap what you hear. How many beats in 'shine'?
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! 'Shine' has just one syllable. The silent E does not add a beat. Now you know!

questions.prompt: How many syllables are in the word **boat**?
questions.hint: When two vowels sit together in a team, they usually make just one sound. Clap once for each vowel sound you hear.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! 'Boat' has just one beat when you clap it. One syllable! Nice work!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Hmm, the two vowels team up to make a single sound. Clap 'boat.' How many beats?
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! 'Boat' has just one syllable. The vowel team makes one beat. Now you know!

questions.prompt: How many syllables are in the word **apple**?
questions.hint: Break it into chunks and say each part out loud: ap - ple. Every chunk needs its own vowel sound.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! 'Ap-ple' has two beats, so it has two syllables. Nice work!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Almost! Clap it out: ap... ple. Count each beat you hear.
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! 'Apple' has two syllables: ap-ple. Now you know!

questions.prompt: Which word has **TWO** syllables? **cake, robin, rain, kite**
questions.hint: Say each word slowly and count the vowel sounds. Watch out for silent-e words and vowel teams that only make one sound.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! 'Robin' claps into two beats: rob-in. That is two syllables. Nice work!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Good try! Clap each word. Three of them have one beat. Which one has two?
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! 'Robin' is the two-syllable word: rob-in. Now you know!

questions.prompt: How many syllables are in the word **cupcake**?
questions.hint: Clap the word in parts: cup - cake. Remember the silent 'e' does not add a beat.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! 'Cup-cake' has two beats, so it has two syllables. Nice work!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Close one! This word is made of smaller words stuck together. Clap each part and count the beats.
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! 'Cupcake' has two syllables: cup-cake. Now you know!

standards.standard_description: Decode two-syllable words following basic patterns by breaking the words into syllables
standards.parent_tip: Split longer words between the two middle consonants: nap-kin, ham-ster.

questions.prompt: How do you break **napkin** into syllables?
questions.hint: Split between the two middle consonants.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! 'Nap-kin' splits between the two middle letters, so each part can be sounded out. Nice work!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Hmm, split the word so each chunk is easy to read. Where do the two middle letters break?
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! The right split is 'nap-kin.' It breaks between the P and K. Now you know!

questions.prompt: How do you break **rabbit** into syllables?
questions.hint: Split between the two B's.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! 'Rab-bit' splits right between the two B's, so each part is easy to read. Nice work!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Almost! When there are two same letters in the middle, split between them. Try again.
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! The right split is 'rab-bit.' It breaks between the two B's. Now you know!

questions.prompt: How should you break the word **pencil** into syllables?
questions.hint: Remember to listen for the natural breaks in the word when you say it slowly.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! 'Pen-cil' splits between the two middle letters, so each chunk is easy to read. Nice work!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Good try! Break the word so each part has a vowel and is easy to say. Where does it split?
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! The right split is 'pen-cil.' It breaks between the N and C. Now you know!

questions.prompt: Which choice shows the word 'happy' correctly broken into its two syllables?
questions.hint: Listen carefully to the two distinct sounds or parts you hear when you say 'happy' slowly.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! 'Hap-py' splits between the two P's, so each part is easy to read. Nice work!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Close one! When two same letters sit in the middle, split right between them. Try again.
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! The right split is 'hap-py.' It breaks between the two P's. Now you know!

questions.prompt: Which word below is broken into two syllables correctly to help a reader sound it out?
questions.hint: Remember that words can be split into parts to help you read them. Look at how each word is divided.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! 'Pi-lot' splits after the first vowel, so the I can say its long name. Nice work!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Take another look! One word is split so each part is easy to sound out. Which one looks right?
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! The right split is 'pi-lot.' The I says its name in the first part. Now you know!

questions.prompt: How do you break the word **insect** into two syllables?
questions.hint: Find the two consonants side-by-side in the middle and split right between them.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! 'In-sect' splits between the two middle letters, so each part is easy to read. Nice work!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Hmm, break the word between the middle letters so each chunk can be sounded out. Try again.
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! The right split is 'in-sect.' It breaks between the N and S. Now you know!

questions.prompt: How do you break the word **muffin** into two syllables?
questions.hint: When two of the same letter sit in the middle, split right between the pair.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! 'Muf-fin' splits right between the two F's, so each part is easy to read. Nice work!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Almost! Two same letters sit in the middle. Split right between them and try again.
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! The right split is 'muf-fin.' It breaks between the two F's. Now you know!

questions.prompt: Which two-syllable word splits between two DIFFERENT middle letters, like **nap-kin**?
questions.hint: Look at the two consonants in the middle of each word — are they the same letter twice, or two different letters?
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! 'Basket' splits between two different middle letters, bas-ket, just like nap-kin. Nice work!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Good try! Which word splits between two DIFFERENT middle letters, not two of the same?
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! 'Basket' is the answer: bas-ket, split between two different letters. Now you know!

questions.prompt: How do you break the word **walnut** into two syllables?
questions.hint: Say it slowly, clap the two parts, and split between the middle consonants.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! 'Wal-nut' splits between the two middle letters, so each part is easy to read. Nice work!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Close one! Break the word between the middle letters so each chunk can be sounded out.
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! The right split is 'wal-nut.' It breaks between the L and N. Now you know!

questions.prompt: Which of these words has TWO syllables? **bench**, **blast**, **scratch**, **kitten**
questions.hint: Clap each word and count the vowel sounds you actually hear — one clap per syllable.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! 'Kitten' claps into two beats, kit-ten, so it has two syllables. Nice work!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Take another look! Clap each word. Three have just one beat. Which one has two?
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! 'Kitten' has two syllables: kit-ten. Now you know!

standards.standard_description: Read words with inflectional endings
standards.parent_tip: -s makes it more than one. -ed means it already happened. -ing means it is happening now.

questions.prompt: What does the **-s** at the end of **cats** mean?
questions.hint: Say it: one cat, two ___? The 's' tells you about how many.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! The -s at the end of 'cats' means there is more than one cat. Nice work!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Hmm, think about one cat and then a whole group of them. What does the -s tell you?
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! The -s means 'more than one.' Cats means lots of cats. Now you know!

questions.prompt: What is the root word in **playing**?
questions.hint: Take off the -ing. What is left?
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! Take off the -ing from 'playing' and you find the root word 'play.' Nice work!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Almost! Cover up the -ing ending. What smaller word is left underneath?
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! The root word is 'play.' Take off -ing and 'play' is left. Now you know!

questions.prompt: When you read the word "walked," what does the ending **-ed** tell you about when the walking happened?
choices: The walking is happening right now.
questions.hint: Think about what **-ed** at the end of a word like "walked" or "jumped" usually means about the action.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! The -ed ending means it already happened, so 'walked' is in the past. Nice work!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Take another look! The -ed ending is a clue about time. Did it happen before, now, or later?
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! The -ed tells us the walking already happened, in the past. Now you know!

questions.prompt: What is the root word (base word) in **running**?
questions.hint: Take off the -ing ending, then remember one of the doubled letters is only there because of the ending.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! Take off the -ning from 'running' and you find the root word 'run.' Nice work!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Good try! Cover up the ending. What small action word is hiding underneath?
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! The root word is 'run.' Take off the ending and 'run' is left. Now you know!

questions.prompt: In which word does the **-ed** ending sound like **/t/**, the way it does in **jumped**?
questions.hint: Say each word out loud and listen only to the very last sound: is it a /t/, a /d/, or an extra 'id' beat?
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! In 'picked' the -ed sounds like /t/, just like in 'jumped.' Nice work!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Almost! Say each word out loud. Which -ed ending sounds like a soft /t/, not a /d/?
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! In 'picked' the -ed makes a /t/ sound, like 'jumped.' Now you know!

questions.prompt: How do you correctly add the **-ed** ending to the word **cry** to make **cried**?
questions.hint: When a word ends in y after a consonant, the y changes before you add the ending.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! For 'cry,' change the y to i and add -ed to make 'cried.' Nice work!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Close one! When a word ends in y, the y changes to i before you add -ed. Try again.
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! The right spelling is 'cried.' Change the y to i, then add -ed. Now you know!

standards.standard_description: Recognize and read grade-appropriate irregularly spelled words
standards.parent_tip: Some words just can't be sounded out. Memorize 'said', 'was', 'were', 'have'.

questions.prompt: Look at these words. Which one is a tricky word?
questions.hint: Remember, tricky words are words that you just have to learn to read by sight because they don't always sound the way they are spelled.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! 'Was' is a tricky word. It does not sound out the way it is spelled. Nice work!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Hmm, three of these sound out just how they look. One does not. Which one surprises you?
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! 'Was' is the tricky word. It sounds like 'wuz,' not how it looks. Now you know!

questions.prompt: Which word says **have**?
questions.hint: Look closely at each word to see which one matches the sound of "have".
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! 'Have' says its name even though it looks like a Magic E word. Nice work!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Almost! Say each word out loud. Which one sounds like 'hav'? Look carefully.
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! The word that says 'have' is spelled h-a-v-e. Now you know!

questions.prompt: Which sentence contains the irregularly spelled word 'were'?
choices: We were going to the park.
questions.correct: We were going to the park.
questions.hint: Look carefully at all the words in each sentence to find 'were'.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! 'We were going to the park' has the tricky word 'were' in it. Nice work!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Take another look! Read each sentence. Which one has the tricky word that sounds like 'wur'?
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! The sentence is 'We were going to the park.' It has the word 'were.' Now you know!

questions.prompt: Which of these words is a tricky word?
questions.hint: Remember, tricky words don't always follow the rules for sounding out.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! 'Said' is a tricky word. It sounds like 'sed,' not how it is spelled. Nice work!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Good try! Three of these sound out just how they look. Which one does not?
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! 'Said' is the tricky word. It sounds like 'sed.' Now you know!

questions.prompt: All of these words start with the /s/ sound. Three of them sound out exactly the way they are spelled. One is a TRICKY word that does NOT sound out that way. Which one is the tricky word?  sand  ·  silk  ·  said  ·  sock
questions.hint: Say each word slowly and blend the letters. Three will match their letters. The one that comes out sounding different is the tricky word.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! 'Said' is the tricky word. It sounds like 'sed,' not how it is spelled. Nice work!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Hmm, three of these sound out just how they look. Which one does not follow the rule?
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! 'Said' is the tricky one. It sounds like 'sed.' Now you know!

questions.prompt: The tricky word that means a person you like and play with is said as 'frend.' Which spelling below is the correct way to write this tricky word?  frend  ·  friend  ·  freind  ·  frinnd
questions.hint: This tricky word has an 'i' hiding before the 'e' that you don't hear when you say it. Sounding it out won't help — you have to remember the order.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! 'Friend' is the right spelling, even though the i is silent. Nice work!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Close one! This tricky word has a sneaky silent i after the r. Which spelling has it?
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! The correct spelling is 'friend.' There is a silent i inside. Now you know!

questions.prompt: Three of these words rhyme with 'nose.' One is a TRICKY word that sounds different even though it looks the same. Which one is the tricky word?  goes  ·  toes  ·  does  ·  hose
questions.hint: Read each one out loud and listen for the odd one. Three will rhyme with each other. The one that sounds like 'duz' is the tricky word.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! 'Does' is the tricky word. It sounds like 'duz,' not like 'nose.' Nice work!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Take another look! Say each word out loud. Which one sounds different from 'nose'?
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! 'Does' is the odd one. It sounds like 'duz,' not 'doze.' Now you know!

questions.prompt: The tricky word in 'Once upon a time' sounds like 'wunce.' Which spelling below is the correct way to write this tricky word?  wonce  ·  wunce  ·  once  ·  onse
questions.hint: You hear a /w/ at the start, but this tricky word does not begin with a 'w'. Remember how it looks, not how it sounds.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! 'Once' is the right spelling, even though it sounds like 'wunce.' Nice work!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Good try! This tricky word starts with an o but sounds like a w. Which spelling looks right?
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! The correct spelling is 'once.' It starts with o but says 'wunce.' Now you know!
