questions.prompt: In three of these words, the 'e' at the end makes the 'i' say its name, the long-i sound like in 'time.' One is a TRICKY word where the 'i' stays short. Which one is the tricky word?  hive  ·  give  ·  dive  ·  five
questions.hint: Try the magic-e rule on each: does the 'i' say its name? Three will. The one where the 'i' stays a short sound is the tricky word.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! In 'give,' the 'i' stays short even with the 'e' on the end. That is the tricky one. Nice work!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Hmm, try saying each word out loud. Three of them rhyme with 'time' and have a long-i. Which one sounds different?
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! The answer is 'give.' The 'e' does not make the 'i' say its name, so it keeps a short sound. Now you know!

standards.standard_description: Read on-level text with purpose and understanding
standards.parent_tip: Before reading, ask: why am I reading this? What do I want to know?

questions.prompt: What is the main reason the author wrote the story about Lily and her ball?
choices: To tell you about a sunny morning.
To teach you how to throw a ball.
To show how Lily solved a problem.
To help you learn about red balls.
questions.correct: To show how Lily solved a problem.
questions.hint: Think about what Lily did when her ball rolled under the bush and how she felt at the end.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! The author wanted to show how Lily solved a problem. That is the big reason for the story. Nice work!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Almost! Think about the WHOLE story, not just one part. What big thing did Lily have to figure out?
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! The author wrote it to show how Lily solved a problem. The story is really about her working it out. Now you know!

questions.prompt: Read the text about butterflies.Butterflies start as small eggs. The eggs hatch into tiny caterpillars. Caterpillars eat many leaves to grow bigger. Then, a caterpillar makes a chrysalis around itself. Inside the chrysalis, it changes. After some time, a butterfly comes out! Butterflies fly and drink sweet nectar from flowers.What is the main reason the author wrote this text?
choices: To make you laugh about caterpillars.
To teach you how butterflies grow.
To help you find butterflies outside.
To tell you a story about a flying butterfly.
questions.correct: To teach you how butterflies grow.
questions.hint: Look closely at what happens to the butterfly from beginning to end in the text.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! The author wrote it to teach you how butterflies grow, step by step. Nice work!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Close one! The text tells all the steps from egg to butterfly. So what is the author trying to help you learn?
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! The author wrote it to teach you how butterflies grow. It shows each step of the change. Now you know!

questions.prompt:
Read the text.
"Dogs need clean water every day. A dog that is thirsty can get sick. Please fill your dog's water bowl each morning."
Why did the author write this?
choices: To tell readers what they should do to care for a dog
To describe what a dog looks like
To tell a funny story about a dog
To explain how dogs drink water
questions.correct: To tell readers what they should do to care for a dog
questions.hint: Look for the sentence that asks the reader to DO something, not just a fact about dogs.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! The author is telling readers what to do to care for a dog. Great thinking!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Good try! The last line says 'Please fill your dog's water bowl.' Is the author telling a story, or telling you to DO something?
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! The author wrote it to tell readers what they should do to care for a dog. It asks you to fill the water bowl. Now you know!

questions.prompt:
Read the text.
"The moon does not make its own light. It shines because the sun's light bounces off it. That is why the moon looks bright at night."
What is the author's main purpose?
choices: To describe how the sun feels warm
To explain why the moon looks bright
To tell a story about the night sky
To make readers laugh about the moon
questions.correct: To explain why the moon looks bright
questions.hint: Ask what the WHOLE text is trying to help you understand, not just one word in it.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! The author wanted to explain why the moon looks bright. It shines from the sun's light. Nice work!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Take another look. The whole text is about the moon and why we see it shine. What is the author explaining?
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! The author wrote it to explain why the moon looks bright. The sun's light bounces off it. Now you know!

questions.prompt:
Read the text.
"First, wash your hands. Next, dry them well. Last, you can eat your snack."
Why did the author write this?
choices: To teach the names of foods
To explain why snacks taste good
To show the steps to do something in order
To tell why hands get dirty
questions.correct: To show the steps to do something in order
questions.hint: Notice the order words: First, Next, Last. What kind of writing uses those?
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! The words 'First, Next, Last' show the steps in order. That is why the author wrote it. Great job!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Hmm, look at the words 'First,' 'Next,' and 'Last.' Those words tell you something is happening in a certain way. What is the author showing?
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! The author wrote it to show the steps to do something in order. The words First, Next, and Last are the clue. Now you know!

questions.prompt:
Read the text.
"Reading is the best thing to do at night. It helps your brain grow. You should read one book before bed."
What does the author want YOU to do?
questions.hint: One sentence tells a fact; another tells you what to do. Find the 'you should' part.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! The author wants YOU to read one book before bed. Great reading!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Almost! The last line tells you to do something. What does the author say YOU should do at night?
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! The author wants you to read one book before bed. The text says it right at the end. Now you know!

questions.prompt:
Read the text.
"Ants are very strong bugs. One ant can lift a leaf much bigger than its body. Ants work together to carry food back home."
Which sentence tells the BIG idea of the whole text?
choices: Ants work together to carry food back home
One ant can lift a leaf much bigger than its body
questions.hint: The big idea is the one all the other sentences give proof for.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! 'Ants are very strong bugs' is the big idea. The other lines just show how strong they are. Nice work!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Good try! One sentence is the BIG idea, and the others are little details that prove it. Which one covers the whole text?
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! The big idea is 'Ants are very strong bugs.' The other lines are just examples of that. Now you know!

standards.standard_description: With prompting and support, read prose and poetry of appropriate complexity for grade 1
standards.parent_tip: Read a little every day. Mix old favorites with new, trickier books.

questions.prompt: After reading the poem 'My Little Dog,' what does the dog in the poem love to do?
choices: eat a big bowl of food
questions.hint: Read the second line of the poem to find out the dog's favorite activity.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! In the poem, the dog loves to chase a bouncy ball. Great remembering!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Hmm, think back to the poem 'My Little Dog.' What fun thing did the dog run after?
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! The dog loves to chase a bouncy ball. The poem tells us that is its favorite thing. Now you know!

questions.prompt:
Read the poem:

Rain taps the glass.
My ball is wet.
My bike is wet.
I sit inside and watch the drops.

How does the child in the poem most likely feel?
choices: Glad it is a sunny day
questions.hint: The poem never says the feeling. Look at where the child is and what her ball and bike are like. What does that tell you?
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! Everything is wet and she is stuck inside, so she feels sad she cannot play. Nice thinking!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Take another look. It is raining and the child sits inside watching the drops. How would that make her feel?
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! The child feels sad she cannot play outside. The rain keeps her stuck inside. Now you know!

questions.prompt:
Read the story:

Sam looked up and saw dark gray clouds. He ran back into his house to grab his coat before walking to the park.

Why did Sam go back for his coat?
choices: He wanted to show his new coat
questions.hint: The story does not say why. Connect what Sam saw in the sky to why someone would need a coat.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! Sam saw dark gray clouds, so he grabbed his coat because he thought it might rain. Great thinking!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Good try! Sam looked up and saw dark gray clouds first. What do dark clouds tell us might happen?
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! Sam went back because he thought it might rain. The dark gray clouds were his clue. Now you know!

questions.prompt:
Read the story:

Mia planted a tiny seed in a pot. Every day she gave it water and set it in the sun. Soon a small green leaf popped up.

What will most likely happen next?
choices: A tall tree will appear right now
questions.hint: Think about the pattern in the story. What has Mia been doing, and what just happened to the seed?
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! Mia waters it and gives it sun, so the plant will keep growing. Nice work!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Almost! A small leaf just popped up and Mia takes good care of it. What happens to a plant that gets water and sun?
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! The plant will keep growing. Mia gives it water and sun every day. Now you know!

questions.prompt:
Read the story:

Ben had two cookies. His friend Tim had none, and Tim looked sad. Ben gave one of his cookies to Tim.

Why did Ben give Tim a cookie?
choices: He had too many to eat
He wanted to make Tim feel better
questions.correct: He wanted to make Tim feel better
questions.hint: The story does not tell you why. Notice how Tim was feeling, then think about why a friend would share.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! Tim looked sad, so Ben gave him a cookie to make him feel better. That was kind! Nice work!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Close one! Tim had no cookies and looked sad. Why would a good friend share with him?
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! Ben gave Tim a cookie because he wanted to make Tim feel better. Tim looked sad. Now you know!

questions.prompt:
Read the poem:

The sun goes down.
The stars come out.
I yawn and close my book.
Mom turns off the light.

What is the child about to do?
questions.hint: No line says the answer. Put several clues together: the sky, the yawn, the closed book, and the light going off.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! The sun is down, the child yawns and closes the book, and Mom turns off the light. Time to sleep! Nice work!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Take another look. It is dark, the child yawns, and the light goes off. What do we do at bedtime?
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! The child is about to go to sleep. The yawn and the light going off are the clues. Now you know!

questions.prompt: Read the poem: "The cat is soft. / The cat is gray. / The cat likes to sleep all day." What color is the cat?
questions.hint: Look at the line that tells the cat's color: "The cat is ___."
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! The poem says 'The cat is gray.' Great reading!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Hmm, look again at the poem. It tells you the cat's color right in the words. What does it say?
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! The cat is gray. The poem says it right in the second line. Now you know!

questions.prompt: Read the poem: "Rain, rain, come and play. / We will splash in you today!" What do the children want to do in the rain?
questions.hint: The last line says "We will ___ in you today."
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! The children want to splash in the rain today. Great reading!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Good try! Read the second line again. What do the children say they will do in the rain?
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! The children want to splash in the rain. The poem says 'We will splash in you today.' Now you know!

questions.prompt: Read the story: "Sam has a red kite. He runs fast. The kite goes up high in the sky." Where does the kite go?
questions.hint: The last sentence tells you where the kite goes.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! The kite goes up high in the sky. Great reading!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Almost! Read the last line again. Where does Sam's kite go when he runs?
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! The kite goes up high in the sky. The story tells us right at the end. Now you know!

questions.prompt: Read the poem: "Little bee, little bee, / Buzzing round the flower tree." What is the little bee doing?
questions.hint: The poem says the bee is "___ round the flower tree."
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! The little bee is buzzing round the flower tree. Great reading!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Take another look at the poem. What sound is the little bee making around the tree?
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! The bee is buzzing. The poem says 'Buzzing round the flower tree.' Now you know!

questions.prompt: Read the story: "Mia is happy. Today is her birthday. She will eat a big cake." How does Mia feel?
questions.hint: The first sentence tells you how Mia feels: "Mia is ___."
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! It is Mia's birthday, so she feels happy. Great reading!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Good try! The story says it is her birthday and she gets cake. How would that make Mia feel?
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! Mia feels happy. The story says 'Mia is happy' because it is her birthday. Now you know!

standards.standard_description: With prompting and support, read informational texts of appropriate complexity for grade 1
standards.parent_tip: Mix in fact books about the child's interests — animals, weather, sports.

questions.prompt: Read the short text. Then answer the question: Based on the text, what is something true about bugs?
choices: Spiders are a type of bug.
questions.hint: Look at the sentences that tell you how many legs different bugs have.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! The text tells us ants have six legs. That one is true. Nice work!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Hmm, think about what the text really told you. Which fact matches the words you read?
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! The true fact is that ants have six legs. The text tells us so. Now you know!

questions.prompt: Based on the passage, what does a tadpole look like?
questions.hint: Look for the sentence in the passage that describes a tadpole.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! The passage says a tadpole looks like a small fish. Nice work!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Good try! Think back to the passage about tadpoles. What did it say a tadpole looks like when it is young?
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! A tadpole looks like a small fish. The passage tells us that. Now you know!

questions.prompt: Read the text: "Bats sleep all day. They hang upside down in dark caves. At night, they fly out to catch bugs to eat." When do bats look for their food?
questions.hint: Find the sentence that tells what bats do at night, and think about what "catch bugs to eat" means.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! Bats sleep all day and fly out at night to catch bugs. So they look for food at night. Nice work!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Take another look. The text says bats sleep all day and fly out later. When do they go catch their bugs?
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! Bats look for food at night. They sleep all day and fly out when it is dark. Now you know!

questions.prompt: Read the text: "A camel can go many days without water. It stores fat in its hump. When food is hard to find, the camel uses the fat to stay alive." Why is the hump helpful to a camel?
choices: It is full of the water the camel drinks.
It holds fat the camel can use when there is no food.
It keeps the camel warm at night.
It helps the camel run very fast.
questions.correct: It holds fat the camel can use when there is no food.
questions.hint: Read the last two sentences together. What is in the hump, and when does the camel use it?
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! The hump holds fat the camel can use when there is no food. Great thinking!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Good try! The text says the hump stores fat and the camel uses it when food is hard to find. So how does the hump help?
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! The hump holds fat the camel can use when there is no food. That keeps it alive. Now you know!

questions.prompt: Read the text: "Bees live together in a hive. Worker bees fly out to find flowers. They bring food back so the baby bees can grow." What would most likely happen if the worker bees stopped flying out?
choices: The baby bees might not get food.
questions.correct: The baby bees might not get food.
questions.hint: The workers bring food back for a reason. What depends on that food?
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! Worker bees bring food to the baby bees, so if they stopped, the baby bees might not get food. Great thinking!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Hmm, the text says worker bees bring food back so baby bees can grow. What would happen to the babies if they stopped?
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! If the worker bees stopped, the baby bees might not get food. The workers are the ones who bring it. Now you know!

questions.prompt: Read the text: "Some trees lose their leaves in the fall. The leaves turn red and gold, then drop to the ground. In spring, tiny green leaves grow back." Which season comes right BEFORE the tree grows new leaves?
questions.hint: The text names two seasons. New leaves grow in spring, so think about the season that comes just before spring.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! The tree loses leaves in fall, and new ones grow in spring. Winter comes right before spring. Nice thinking!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Almost! New leaves grow in spring. Think about the seasons in order. Which cold season comes right before spring?
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! The answer is winter. Winter comes right before spring, when the new leaves grow. Now you know!

questions.prompt: Read the text: "A turtle carries its shell on its back. When a fox comes near, the turtle pulls its head and legs inside. The hard shell keeps the turtle safe." Why does the turtle pull inside its shell when a fox comes?
choices: Because the shell keeps it safe from the fox
Because the shell is too heavy to carry
Because it wants to take a nap
Because it is looking for food
questions.correct: Because the shell keeps it safe from the fox
questions.hint: Read the first and last sentences together. What does the shell do, and why does that matter when a fox is near?
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! The turtle pulls inside because its hard shell keeps it safe from the fox. Great thinking!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Good try! The text says the hard shell keeps the turtle safe. When a fox comes near, what is the turtle trying to do?
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! The turtle pulls inside because the shell keeps it safe from the fox. Now you know!

standards.standard_description: Demonstrate command of the conventions of standard English grammar and usage when writing or speaking
standards.parent_tip: Nouns name things. Verbs show action. Point them out in everyday sentences.

questions.prompt: Which word is a **noun** (a thing or person)?
questions.hint: A NOUN names a person, place, or thing. Three of these are actions or describing words.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! 'Dog' is a noun because it names a thing. Nice work!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Hmm, a noun names a person, place, or thing. Which word names a thing you can see?
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! The noun is 'dog.' It names a thing, so it is a noun. Now you know!

questions.prompt: Which word is a **verb** (an action)?
questions.hint: A VERB is an action — something you DO.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! 'Runs' is a verb because it is an action. Nice work!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Good try! A verb is an action word, something you can DO. Which word shows an action?
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! The verb is 'runs.' It is an action word, so it is a verb. Now you know!

questions.prompt: Choose the word that best completes the sentence: The birds ___ flying in the sky.
questions.hint: Think about if 'birds' means one bird or many birds. The verb needs to match the number of birds.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! 'The birds are flying' is correct. We use 'are' for more than one. Nice work!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Almost! The sentence has many birds, more than one. Which word matches when we talk about a bunch of birds?
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! The answer is 'are.' We say 'the birds are flying' because there is more than one bird. Now you know!

questions.prompt: Which sentence is written correctly? Read each one carefully.
choices: The two dogs run to the park.
The two dogs running to the park.
The two dogs runs to the park.
The two dog run to the park.
questions.correct: The two dogs run to the park.
questions.hint: When the naming word means more than one, the action word usually drops the -s. Check that the naming word is plural too.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! 'The two dogs run to the park' is correct. Two dogs need 'run,' not 'runs.' Nice work!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Good try! Read each one out loud. There are TWO dogs, so which sentence matches its words the right way?
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! The right sentence is 'The two dogs run to the park.' Two dogs go with 'run.' Now you know!

questions.prompt: Finish the sentence with the correct word: "There are three little ___ hiding in the barn."
questions.hint: Most words add -s to mean more than one, but a few change in a special way. Say the word for many out loud.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! 'Three little mice' is correct. 'Mice' is the special word for more than one mouse. Nice work!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Hmm, there are three of them, more than one. One mouse changes to a special word when there are many. Which word is it?
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! The answer is 'mice.' More than one mouse are called mice. Now you know!

questions.prompt: Choose the word that correctly finishes the sentence: "Yesterday I ___ my whole lunch at school."
questions.hint: The clue word 'Yesterday' means it already happened. Some action words don't just add -ed for the past.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! 'Yesterday I ate my lunch' is correct. 'Ate' is the word for the past. Nice work!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Take another look. The word 'yesterday' means it already happened. Which word tells about the past the right way?
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! The answer is 'ate.' We say 'yesterday I ate' because it already happened. Now you know!

questions.prompt: Which word correctly finishes the sentence? "My friend and ___ went to the playground."
questions.hint: Try the sentence with just that word alone: "___ went to the playground." Which one sounds right by itself?
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! 'My friend and I went' is correct. We use 'I' when it is doing the action. Nice work!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Good try! Try dropping 'My friend and' and say it alone. Which word sounds right doing the going?
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! The answer is 'I.' We say 'My friend and I went to the playground.' Now you know!

questions.prompt: Which connecting word makes the sentence make sense? "I put on my warm coat ___ it was very cold outside."
questions.hint: Ask yourself WHY the coat went on. Pick the word that gives the reason.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! 'Because' fits. The coat and the cold go together with 'because.' Nice thinking!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Almost! The cold is the REASON she put on the coat. Which joining word shows a reason?
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! The answer is 'because.' It shows the cold was the reason for the warm coat. Now you know!

standards.standard_description: Demonstrate command of the conventions of standard English capitalization, punctuation, and spelling when writing
standards.parent_tip: Two simple rules: sentences start with a capital and end with a mark. People's names always capital.

questions.prompt: Which sentence is written correctly?
questions.hint: Look for the capital letter at the start AND the period at the end.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! 'The dog runs fast.' has a capital at the start and a period at the end. Nice work!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Good try! A good sentence starts with a capital letter AND ends with a period. Which one has both?
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! The correct one is 'The dog runs fast.' It starts with a capital and ends with a period. Now you know!

questions.prompt: Which sentence ends with the correct mark?
questions.hint: Think about what kind of sentence each one is and what punctuation mark it needs at the very end.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! 'The dog barked.' ends with the right mark, a period. Nice work!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Hmm, this is a telling sentence, not a question or a shout. Which mark belongs at the end of a telling sentence?
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! The answer is 'The dog barked.' A telling sentence ends with a period. Now you know!

questions.prompt: Which name is written correctly?
questions.hint: Names always start with a capital letter!
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! 'Maya' is written right. Names start with ONE capital letter. Nice work!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Take another look. A name has a capital at the START, and the rest are little letters. Which one looks right?
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! The right one is 'Maya.' A name gets one capital letter at the start. Now you know!

questions.prompt: Which sentence correctly uses the ending mark for a question?
questions.hint: Think about what mark you put at the end when you are asking something.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! 'What is your name?' ends with a question mark. Nice work!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Good try! This sentence is ASKING something. Which mark do we use at the end of a question?
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! The answer is 'What is your name?' A question ends with a question mark. Now you know!

questions.prompt: Fix this: **ben and maya play.** What is wrong?
questions.hint: Look at the words 'ben' and 'maya.' What kind of words always start with capitals?
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! Names need capitals, so 'ben' and 'maya' should start with big letters. Nice thinking!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Hmm, look at 'ben' and 'maya.' Those are people's names. What is missing from the start of each one?
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! The problem is that names need capitals. 'Ben' and 'Maya' should start with big letters. Now you know!

questions.prompt: Which sentence uses commas correctly to list the three things? "We bought milk, eggs, and bread."
choices: We bought milk, eggs, and bread.
We bought milk, eggs and bread,
We bought milk eggs, and bread.
We bought, milk, eggs, and bread.
questions.correct: We bought milk, eggs, and bread.
questions.hint: A comma goes between each thing in the list, but not right after the action word or at the very end.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! 'We bought milk, eggs, and bread.' uses commas right to list the three things. Nice work!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Good try! When we list things, a comma goes after each one before 'and.' Which sentence has the commas in the right spots?
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! The correct one is 'We bought milk, eggs, and bread.' The commas go between the listed things. Now you know!

questions.prompt: Which line writes this date correctly? (the fourth day of July, 2026)
questions.hint: The month name is special and needs a capital, and one comma goes between the day and the year.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! 'July 4, 2026' is right. The month has a capital, and a comma goes before the year. Nice work!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Take another look. A month starts with a capital letter, and we put a comma before the year. Which line does both?
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! The answer is 'July 4, 2026.' Capital month and a comma before the year. Now you know!

questions.prompt: Maria wants to ask what time it is. Which sentence writes her question correctly?
questions.hint: A question starts with a capital letter and ends with a mark that shows you are asking.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! 'What time is it?' starts with a capital and ends with a question mark. Nice work!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Almost! She is ASKING a question, so it needs a capital at the start and the right end mark. Which one is written right?
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! The answer is 'What time is it?' A capital at the start and a question mark at the end. Now you know!

questions.prompt: Which sentence is written correctly? (It is about a friend named Sam.)
choices: my friend Sam has a red bike.
my friend sam has a red bike.
My friend sam has a red bike.
My friend Sam has a red bike.
questions.correct: My friend Sam has a red bike.
questions.hint: Two words here need a capital letter: the very first word of the sentence and the person's name.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! 'My friend Sam has a red bike.' has a capital at the start AND on the name Sam. Nice work!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Good try! The first word AND the name both need a capital letter. Which sentence has capitals in both spots?
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! The right one is 'My friend Sam has a red bike.' The start and the name Sam both get capitals. Now you know!

questions.prompt: Which word is spelled correctly? "I stayed inside ___ it was raining."
questions.hint: Sound it out slowly, then remember this common word has an 'au' in the middle and ends in 'se'.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! 'Because' is spelled the right way. Great spelling!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Hmm, look closely at each choice and read the letters slowly. Which one is spelled the right way?
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! The correct spelling is 'because.' Now you know!
